style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders text in small <br />font.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>STRIKE and S</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders strikethrough <br />text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>U</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders underlined <br />text.</td></tr></tbody></table><ul><li>TT <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</li><li>I <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li><li>B <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li><li>BIG <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font size=”5”&gt;</li><li>SMALL <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font size=”1”&gt;</li><li>STRIKE and S <br />This tag will be rendered in a strikethrough text.</li><li>U  <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li></ul><h2>Phrase elements</h2><p>The table below lists assorted Phrase elements:</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Tag <br />name </th><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Function </th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> EM</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Indicates emphasis.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> STRONG</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Indicates stronger emphasis.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> CITE</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Contains a citation or a <br />reference to other sources.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>DFN </strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><p>Indicates that this is the defining <br />instance of the enclosed term.</p></td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>CODE</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Designates a fragment of computer <br />code.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>SAMP</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Designates a sample output from <br />programs, scripts, etc.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>KBD</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates the text to be entered <br />by the user.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>VAR</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an instance of a variable <br />or program argument.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>ABBR</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an abbreviated form such <br />as WWW, HTTP, URI, and Mass.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>ACRONYM</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an acronym such as <br />WAC and radar.</td></tr></tbody></table><ul><li><p><em>EM</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>STRONG</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;b&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>CITE</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>DFN</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>CODE</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>SAMP</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>KBD</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>VAR</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>ABBR</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as normal text.</p></li><li><p><em>ACRONYM</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as normal text.</p></li></ul><h2>Ordered and unordered lists and list Item tags</h2><p>Ordered and unordered lists are rendered in an identical manner; however, ordered list items are numbered.</p><p>The report engine supports both unordered and ordered lists without attributes. The list tag attributes will be ignored in the report output. The list tag attributes are type, start, value, and compact.</p><p>Neither unordered nor ordered lists are supported in <em>XLSX</em> and <em>ODS</em> templates.</p><h3>Ordered lists</h3><p>An Ordered List is defined by the &lt;OL&gt; element. This element contains one or more &lt;LI&gt; elements that define the actual items of the list.</p><p>Unlike unordered lists (UL), items in an ordered list have a definite sequence. A conversion will render each item in the list with a number. All &lt;OL&gt; attributes will be ignored in the report output. An example of an Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Ordered List Tag" ac:alt="Sample of Ordered List Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ordered list tag sample.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html14 - OL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Nested ordered lists</h3><p>HTML conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting. Each level will begin at 1. An example of the Nested Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Nested OL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Nested OL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedOL1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> As shown in the figure below, the outputs in<em> RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html18 - NestedOL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p><h3>Unordered lists</h3><p>An Unordered List is defined by the UL element. This element contains one or more LI elements that define the actual list items.</p><p>A conversion will render the UL element with a bullet preceding each list item. All UL attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of an Unordered List tag:</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Sample of UL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of UL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Unordered list tag sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br />As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em>, or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><h3 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html06 - UL2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h3><h3><br />Nested unordered lists</h3><p>Lists can also be nested. <em>HTML</em> conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting.</p><p><em>HTML</em> conversion should attempt to present a small filled-in circle to the first level, a small circle outline to the second level, and a filled-in square to the third level. Bullets after the third level are filled-in squares.  An example of the Nested Unordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Nested UL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Nested UL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUL1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in<em> RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html10 - NestedUL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />  </p><h2>Definition list tags</h2><p>A Definition List is defined by the DL element. An entry in the list is created using the DT element for the term being defined and the DD element for the definition of the term.</p><p>A definition list can have multiple terms for a given definition, as well as multiple definitions for a given term. Authors can also give a term without a corresponding definition, and vice versa, but such a structure rarely makes sense.</p><p>A conversion will render DT as a non-indent item and DD as a single indent item. The Definition List tag is not supported in the<em> XLSX</em> template. The figure below shows an example of a Definition List tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of DL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of DL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sample DL tags.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><h2><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html22 - DL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br />Line and paragraph tags</h2><p>A line break is defined by the &lt;BR&gt; element. This element inserts a single line break. It is an empty tag, meaning that it has no end tag. The line attributes will be ignored in the report output.</p><p>A paragraph is defined by the &lt;P&gt; element. The element automatically creates some space before and after itself. The paragraph attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of a Line Break and Paragraph tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Line and Paragraph Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Line and Paragraph Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html25 - LineAndParagraph1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html26 - LineAndParagraph2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Preformatted text</h2><p>A preformatted text is defined by the &lt;PRE&gt; element. All the space and carriage returns are rendered exactly as you type them. The preformatted attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of the preformatted text:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Preformatted Text" ac:alt="Sample of Preformatted Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html29 - Preformatted1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF/ ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html30 - PRE2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html32 - PRE4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Heading tags</h2><p>A heading is defined by the &lt;H1&gt;, &lt;H2&gt;, &lt;H3&gt;, &lt;H4&gt;, &lt;H5&gt;, or &lt;H6&gt; element. In this report, all heading tags will be rendered as &lt;b&gt; for<em> ODT, RTF,</em> and <em>OOXML</em> document outputs. The heading attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of Heading tags.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Heading Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Heading Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html33 - HEADING1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html34 - HEADING2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html36 - HEADING4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Link tags</h2><p>A Link tag is defined by the &lt;A&gt; element. This element is used to create a link to another document with the href attribute. The href attribute specifies the destination of the link. Link tags are not supported in the <em>XLSX</em> template. The figure below shows an example of a Link tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Link Tag" ac:alt="Sample of Link Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html37 - LINK1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following three figures, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em>, and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html38 - LINK2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html39 - LINK3.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html40 - LINK4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Table tags</h2><p>A table is defined by the &lt;TABLE&gt; element. A table consists of multi-dimensional data arranged in rows and columns.</p><h3>Table elements</h3><p>The &lt;TABLE&gt; element takes a number of optional attributes to provide presentational alternatives in a document. The table attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>border</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Specifies the width in unit of the border of a table.</p><ul><li>bgcolor</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Specifies the table background color. Applying the background color here will affect the whole table.</p><p><br /></p><p>Table elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p>The figure below shows an example of table tags:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Table Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Table Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html41 - Table1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html42 - Table2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html44 - Table4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Border</strong></p><p>Border width in <em>HTML</em> is specified in pixels. When the table attributes are converted into <em>RTF</em>, <em>ODF</em>, or <em>OOXML</em>, 1 pixel will be equal to 1 pt.</p><p><strong>Color</strong></p><p>The attribute value type &quot;bgcolor&quot; refers to color definitions as specified in [SRGB]. A color value may be either a hexadecimal number (prefixed by a hash mark) or one of the following sixteen colors. Colors are case-insensitive.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The following table lists common Table Element Colors.</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Color</th><th style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Hexadecimal <br />Code</th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Black</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#00000000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Silver</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#C0C0C0</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Gray</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#808080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">White</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FFFFFF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Maroon</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#800000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Red</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FF0000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Purple</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#800080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Fuchsia</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FF00FF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Green</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#008000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Lime</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#00FF00</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Olive</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#808000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Yellow</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FFFF00</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Navy</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#000080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Blue</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#0000FF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Teal</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#008080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Aqua</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#00FFFF</td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Row elements</h3><p>The &lt;TR&gt; elements act as a container for a row of table cells. The &lt;TR&gt; elements must be contained within a &lt;TABLE&gt;.</p><p>&lt;TR&gt; contains &lt;TH&gt; or &lt;TD&gt; elements, which in turn contain the actual data of the table. &lt;TR&gt; takes presentational attributes for specifying the alignment of cells within the row and the row's background color. The row attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align - Specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in a row.</li><li>valign - Specifies the vertical position of a cell's content.  </li><li>bgcolor - Specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to rows only (see Color in Table Elements for more details).</li></ul><p>Row elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS</em>, and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p><strong>Align</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the alignment of data and the justification of text in a cell. Possible values are:</p><ul><li>left -  Left-flushed data/Left-justified text. This is the default value for table data.</li><li>center - Centered data/Center-justified text. This is the default value for table headers.</li><li>right - Right-flushed data/Right-justified text.</li><li>justify - Double -justified text.</li><li>char - No text alignment set.</li></ul><p><strong>Valign</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the vertical position of data within a cell. Possible values are:</p><ul><li>top: Cell data is flush with the top of a cell.</li><li>middle: Cell data is centered vertically within a cell. This is the default value.</li><li>bottom: Cell data is flush with the bottom of a cell.</li><li>baseline: No text alignment set.</li></ul><p>The figure below shows an example of TR tags:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TR Tags" ac:alt="Sample of TR Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html45 - TR1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html46 - TR2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html48 - TR4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Cell elements</h3><p>The &lt;TD&gt; elements define a data cell in a table. &lt;TD&gt; elements are contained within a &lt;TR&gt; element (a table row). The cell attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align  <br />specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.</li><li>valign <br />specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details. </li><li>bgcolor <br />specifies the table background color. A background color will apply only to cells. See Color in Table Elements for more details.</li><li>rowspan <br />rows spanned by the cell</li><li>colspan <br />columns spanned by the cell</li></ul><p>Cell elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p><strong>Row span</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the number of rows spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one (&quot;1&quot;). For an <em>RTF</em> output, the result of row span (<em>*.rtf</em>) is readable only in Word on Mac, and MS Word.</p><p><strong>Column span</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the number of columns spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one (&quot;1&quot;). The figure below shows an example of a column span:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TD Tags As Column Spans" ac:alt="Sample of TD Tags As Column Spans"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html49 - TD1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html50 - TD2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html52 - TD4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Column width<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> </span></h3><p>Table column width can be adjusted via attributes and stylesheet. For example, a column's width can be set by its width attribute. Percentage or pixel units are allowed for each &lt;TD&gt; element. You may set table width in &lt;table&gt; tags by absolute pixel value or percentage of page ratio, then allocate width from the table to each column later.</p><div><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><p><br /></p><p>For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2f40341f-c2bc-43a3-b794-d695a88ed1c8"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Using attributes:
````html
<table width="500px">
        <tr>
                <td width="25%">25%</td>
                <td width="75%">75%</td>
       </tr>
</table>
<table width="500px">
        <tr>
                <td width="400px">400px</td>
                <td width="100px">100px</td>
        </tr>
</table> 


<table>
	<tr>
		<td width="30">30px</td>
		<td width="60">60px</td>
		<td width="90">90px</td>
	</tr>
</table>
 
<table>
	<tr>
		<td width="25%">25%</td>
		<td width="75%">75%</td>
	</tr>
</table>



Using Stylesheet:
<table style="width:50%">

	<tr>
		<td style="width:70%">70%</td>
		<td style="width:30%">30%</td>
	</tr>
</table>

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div></div><div><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><p><br /></p></div><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">DOCX, RTF and ODT templates retain their width values. </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">The column width in the report is the same as defined in the Advanced HTML Editor dialog.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">If the defined values exceed the paper margins, the table column widths will be converted to a percentage format so that the table appears within the page margins.</span></p></div></div><h3>Header elements</h3><p>The &lt;TH&gt; elements define a header cell in a table. &lt;TH&gt; elements are contained within a &lt;TR&gt; element (a table row). The header attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align <br />specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.</li><li>valign <br />specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details. </li><li>bgcolor <br />specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to the whole table. See Color in Table Elements for more details.</li><li>rowspan <br />rows spanned by the cell. See Row span in Cell Elements for more details.</li><li>colspan <br />columns spanned by the cell. See Column span in Cell Elements for more details.</li></ul><p>The default alignment for &lt;TH&gt; is center and the default font style for &lt;TH&gt; is bold. Header elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates. The figure below shows an example of header elements:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TH Tags As Header Elements" ac:alt="Sample of TH Tags As Header Elements"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html53 - TH1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The following two figures show the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML,</em> respectively.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html54 - TH2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html56 - TH4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Image tags</h2><p>The &lt;img&gt; tag embeds an image in a document. The &lt;img&gt; tag consists of four attributes: <strong>src</strong>, <strong>width</strong>, <strong>height</strong>, and<strong> alt</strong>.</p><h3>src</h3><p>The src attribute specifies the location of an image resource. The value of this attribute can be one of the following types:</p><ul><li>A URL. The recognized scheme types are <em>HTTP, HTTPS</em>, and <em>FILE</em></li><li>An absolute path such as<em> c:/user/image.png</em>. The path separator can be either /(slash) or \(blackslash) </li></ul><p>The output image format will depend on the format of the image source.</p><h3>width</h3><p>The width attribute specifies the width of an image in pixel units. For example, width = “100” or width = “100px”</p><h3>height</h3><p>The <strong>height</strong> attribute specifies the height of an image in pixel units. For example, height = “100”, or height = “100px”</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="629bf1ba-19df-4333-8d08-5f0d2dd35c2a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the width or height attribute of an image is not specified, the size of the image will be calculated according to the following rules:</p><ul><li>For an image file that contains the width and height properties, such as <em>JPG, PNG,</em> and <em>GIF</em>, the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the image. </li><li>For an image file that has no width and height properties, such as <em>SVG, EMF,</em> and <em>WMF</em>, the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the paper.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>alt</h3><p>The <strong>alt</strong> attribute specifies the alternate text of an image in case the image cannot be displayed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f6493199-6b6b-485a-af51-db66ad772ff9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul class="alternate"><li>The alternate text of image tags in DOCX, XLSX, and PPTX is available only in Microsoft Office.</li><li>The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is shown only in the Description field, rather than the Title field in Microsoft Word and OpenOffice Writer.</li><li>The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is not supported in LibreOffice.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>In the event that the HTML code is as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8a88872e-6b09-49ee-8137-117fa047e987"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<img src="file:/D:/Waterlilies.jpg" width="200" height="200" alt="Waterlilies"/>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>The image will appear as shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of IMG Tag" ac:alt="Sample of IMG Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_img_2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Superscript and subscript tags</h2><h3>Superscript</h3><p>The &lt;sup&gt; tag defines a supersript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed above the line. Superscript characters can be used to write footnotes, like www<sup>[1]</sup>, for example:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sample of superscript.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Superscript output.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Subscript</h3><p>The &lt;sub&gt; tag defines a subscript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed below the line. For example, subscript characters can be used to write chemical formulas, such as H2O:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sample of subscript.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Output of subscript.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715756 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01225: Supported scripting languages

- page_id: `136715756`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715756/Supported+scripting+languages
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2023-09-22T06:29:58.739+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

1425328535

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

1425328548

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

1425328537

Macro Engine supports five scripting languages: BeanShell, JavaScript Rhino, Jython, Groovy, and JRuby.

**BeanShell** 
BeanShell is a lightweight scripting language for Java. The shipped version is 2.1.7. The advantage of using BeanShell is that its syntax is compatible with Java; therefore, you can use the code assistant feature in most Java IDE. The BeanShell syntax documentation is available at *[http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html](http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html)*.

**JavaScript** 
JavaScript is a scripting language based on Java syntax. The documentation is available at: 
*[http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html)* and *[https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation](https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation)*.

#### WARNING: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

The JavaScript Nashorn language has been removed from the modeling tool. Please use JavaScript Rhino instead. Learn more on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn' space=''] page.

**Jython** 
Jython, a successor of JPython, is a Python programming language implemented in Java. The documentation is available at *[http://wiki.python.org/jython](http://wiki.python.org/jython)*. Besides automating MagicDraw by using Jython scripting, the plugin development in the Jython programming language is also supported in MagicDraw. For more information on Jython, see JPython Scripting in MagicDraw OpenAPI User Guide.

**Groovy** 
Groovy is an agile and dynamic language for the Java Virtual Machine. Macro Engine uses Groovy 2.0.1. The advantage of using Groovy is that its Java-like syntax seamlessly integrates with the existing Java source code and libraries. It supports many IDEs that provide code completion and debugging. BeanShell scripts can be easily moved to Groovy, with some modifications. For more information on Groovy, go to *[http://groovy-lang.org/](http://groovy-lang.org/)*.

**JRuby** 
JRuby is a 100% pure-Java implementation of the Ruby programming language. The documentation is available at *[http://jruby.org/documentation](http://jruby.org/documentation)*.

By default, JRuby is not included in the modeling tool, however, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding JRuby and installing Gems' space=''].

1425328534

**Related pages**

****

- Using code completion to develop BeanShell scripts
- Using NetBeans IDE to develop Groovy Scripts
- Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts
- Installing Gems for JRuby
- Adding a scripting language to MagicDraw

The documentation is available at:<br /><em><a href="http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html">http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html</a></em> and <em><a href="https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation">https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation</a></em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a75c8014-dd4d-4ec1-a8e1-f66e742a2bff"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The JavaScript Nashorn language has been removed from the modeling tool. The documentation is available at <em><a class="external-link" href="http://jruby.org/documentation" rel="nofollow">http://jruby.org/documentation</a></em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aef3b1ca-54cf-4c05-bd0b-539b04b3a8e0"><ac:rich-text-body><p>By default, JRuby is not included in the modeling tool, however, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding JRuby and installing Gems" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[you can add it manually]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e6acb6c-058c-497e-a088-e0a990820b56"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1425328534</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+code+completion+to+develop+BeanShell+scripts">Using code completion to develop BeanShell scripts</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+NetBeans+IDE+to+develop+Groovy+Scripts">Using NetBeans IDE to develop Groovy Scripts</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+Eclipse+to+develop+Groovy+scripts">Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Installing+Gems+for+JRuby">Installing Gems for JRuby</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Adding+a+scripting+language+to+MagicDraw">Adding a scripting language to MagicDraw</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715347 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01226: Supported UML Specification Changes from Version 2.3 to 2.4.1

- page_id: `136715347`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715347/Supported+UML+Specification+Changes+from+Version+2.3+to+2.4.1
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:24.115+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Plugins migration > Plugins migration to MagicDraw 17.0.1 and later Open API
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Supported UML specification changes include:

44

###### Metamodel Changes

| Metamodel Change | Description / Comment |
| --- | --- |
| DATA TYPES ADDED |  |
| Real | Added to the PrimitiveTypes package. You can now use values of a real type in your models. |
| METACLASSES ADDED |  |
| LiteralReal | Specifies the real value. |
| METACLASSES CHANGED |  |
| DestructionEvent → DestructionOccurrenceSpecification | The DestructionEvent class has been renamed to DestructionOccurrenceSpecification. It represents the destruction of an instance described by the lifeline that contains this instance.DestructionOccurrenceSpecification is a specialization of MessageOccurrenceSpecification. |
| METACLASSES REMOVED |  |
| ExecutionEvent | These metaclasses have been removed from the Interactions package due to unsupported event types. |
| CreationEvent |  |
| ReceiveOperationEvent |  |
| ReceiveSignalEvent |  |
| SendOperationEvent |  |
| SendSignalEvent |  |
| PROPERTIES ADDED |  |
| Package::URI:String[0..1] {id} | Provides the package with an identifier that can be used for many purposes. URI is the universally unique identification of the package following the IETF URI specification, RFC 2396 http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2396.txt and it must comply with those syntax rules. You can now specify this property for packages, profiles, and models. For the notation of the URI property, see . |
| Property::isID:Boolean=False | Indicates that the property can be used to uniquely identify an instance of the containing class, when the value is set to true. The default value is false. For the notation of the isID property modifier, see . |
| InteractionUse::returnValue:ValueSpecification [0..1] | Specifies the value returned by the executed interaction. |
| InteractionUse::returnValueRecipient:Property [0..1] | Specifies the recipient of the value which is returned by the executed interaction. |
| EnumerationLiteral::/classifier:Enumaration[1] | The classifier of this EnumerationLiteral should now be equal to the enumeration that contains this enumeration literal. Redefines InstanceSpecification::classifier. |
| ASSOCIATIONS REMOVED |  |
| ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification::event: ExecutionEvent[1] | The association from ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification to ExecutionEvent has been removed, as the ExecutionEvent metaclass does not exist in the UML 2.4.1 |
| OccurenceSpecification::event:Event[1] | The specification of the occurring event is not referenced any more. |
| BehavioredClassifier::ownedTrigger:Trigger[0..*] | Trigger descriptions owned by a classifier are not referenced any more. |
| SUBSETTING CHANGES |  |
| Interface::redefinedInterface {subsets redefinedElement → redefinedClassifier} | An interface now references all the interfaces that are redefined by this interface. |
| SUBSETTING ADDEDNote: These features were already available in earlier versions of MagicDraw. |  |
| Duration::expr:ValueSpecification[0..1] {subsets Element::ownedElement} | Element or elements can be selected or created only under the owned element’s scope. |
| LinkEndData::qualifier:QualifierValue[*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| LinkAction::endData:LinkEndData[2..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| CreateLinkAction::endData:LinkEndCreationData[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| DestroyLinkAction::endData:LinkEndDestructionData[2..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| TimeExpression::expr:ValueSpecification[0..1] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| ValuePin::value:ValueSpecification[1] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| State::deferrableTrigger:Trigger[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| StructuredActivityNode::edge:ActivityEdge[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| StructuredActivityNode::node:ActivityNode[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| ValueSpecificationAction::value: ValueSpecification[1] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| AcceptEventAction::trigger:Triger[1..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| Stereotype::icon:Image[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| TimeEvent::when:TimeExpression[1] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| InteractionUse::argument:ValueSpecification[*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| StructuredActivityNode::node:ActivityNode [0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| SequenceNode::executableNode: ExecutableNode[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| Transition::trigger:Trigger[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement} |  |
| Classifier::/feature {subsets Namespace::member} | The /feature property of Classifier is now included as a part of the member property. |
| Feature::/featuringClassifier {subsets NamedElement::memberNamespace} | The /featuringClassifier property of Feature is now included as a part of the memberNamespace property. |
| Property::owningAssociation {subsets RedefinableElement::redefinitionContext} | A property now references the owning association of this property. |
| DEFAULT VALUE CHANGES |  |
| DurationObservation::firstEvent:Boolean[0..2] =True | The default value has been removed. In effect, the value of the firstEvent property is now by default empty. |
| DurationConstraint::firstEvent:Boolean[0..2] =True |  |
| PackageableElement::visibility:VisibilityKind[1] =False → Public | The default value of this property has been changed to Public.This means that packageable elements are now by default public. |
| MULTIPLICITY CHANGES |  |
| EnumerationLiteral::enumeration:Enumeration [0..1] → [1] | The Enumeration must now be specified. It has become mandatory. |
| Multiplicity of ExecutionSpecification association with ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification: [1] → [0..2] | ExecutionSpecification is now associated with two ExecutionOccurrenceSpecifications:The start ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification that designates the start of an action or behavior.The finish ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification that designates the finish of an action or behavior. |
| DERIVATION CHANGES |  |
| Constraint::/context → context | The context property of the constraint is no longer derived. It is now a settable property. |
| Message::/signature → signature | The signature of the message is no longer derived. However, it remains read-only. |

###### Notation changes

1. If there is a value defined for the URI property of a package, model, or profile, it is automatically displayed on the corresponding shape. The following figure depicts an example of the package notation with the URI property value defined: 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. If the isID property is set to true for the selected attribute, the {id} modifier is displayed in the property modifiers group on the class shape. The following figure depicts an example of the attribute notation when the isID property is set to true: 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. The name of a synchronous message is now displayed on a diagram pane, even if the message does not have a signal or an operation assigned. The following figure depicts an example of the synchronous message name notation: 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Brackets “()” are no more added to a call message name if the message does not have a signal or an operation assigned. The following figure depicts an example of the call message name notation: 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>Supported UML specification changes include:</h3><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="170747f4-008e-4e01-8a1b-db29bbb3d7ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><h4 class="bold">Metamodel Changes</h4><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><strong>Metamodel Change</strong></th><th><strong>Description / Comment</strong></th></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>DATA TYPES ADDED</strong></td></tr><tr><td>Real</td><td>Added to the PrimitiveTypes package. You can now use values of a real type in your models.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>METACLASSES ADDED</strong></td></tr><tr><td>LiteralReal</td><td>Specifies the real value.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>METACLASSES CHANGED</strong></td></tr><tr><td>DestructionEvent → DestructionOccurrenceSpecification</td><td>The DestructionEvent class has been renamed to DestructionOccurrenceSpecification. It represents the destruction of an instance described by the lifeline that contains this instance.<br />DestructionOccurrenceSpecification is a specialization of MessageOccurrenceSpecification.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>METACLASSES REMOVED</strong></td></tr><tr><td>ExecutionEvent</td><td colspan="1" rowspan="6">These metaclasses have been removed from the Interactions package due to unsupported event types.</td></tr><tr><td>CreationEvent</td></tr><tr><td>ReceiveOperationEvent</td></tr><tr><td>ReceiveSignalEvent</td></tr><tr><td>SendOperationEvent</td></tr><tr><td>SendSignalEvent</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>PROPERTIES ADDED</strong></td></tr><tr><td>Package::URI:String[0..1] {id}</td><td>Provides the package with an identifier that can be used for many purposes. URI is the universally unique identification of the package following the IETF URI specification, RFC 2396 <a href="http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2396.txt">http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2396.txt</a> and it must comply with those syntax rules. You can now specify this property for packages, profiles, and models. For the notation of the URI property, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Notation changes"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Notation Changes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported UML Specification Changes from Version 2.3 to 2.4.1" /></ac:link>.</td></tr><tr><td>Property::isID:Boolean=False</td><td>Indicates that the property can be used to uniquely identify an instance of the containing class, when the value is set to true. The default value is false. For the notation of the isID property modifier, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Notation changes"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Notation Changes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported UML Specification Changes from Version 2.3 to 2.4.1" /></ac:link>.</td></tr><tr><td>InteractionUse::returnValue:ValueSpecification [0..1]</td><td>Specifies the value returned by the executed interaction.</td></tr><tr><td>InteractionUse::returnValueRecipient:Property [0..1]</td><td>Specifies the recipient of the value which is returned by the executed interaction.</td></tr><tr><td>EnumerationLiteral::/classifier:Enumaration[1]</td><td>The classifier of this EnumerationLiteral should now be equal to the enumeration that contains this enumeration literal. Redefines InstanceSpecification::classifier.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>ASSOCIATIONS REMOVED</strong></td></tr><tr><td>ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification::event: ExecutionEvent[1]</td><td>The association from ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification to ExecutionEvent has been removed, as the ExecutionEvent metaclass does not exist in the UML 2.4.1</td></tr><tr><td>OccurenceSpecification::event:Event[1]</td><td>The specification of the occurring event is not referenced any more.</td></tr><tr><td>BehavioredClassifier::ownedTrigger:Trigger[0..*]</td><td>Trigger descriptions owned by a classifier are not referenced any more.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>SUBSETTING CHANGES</strong></td></tr><tr><td>Interface::redefinedInterface {subsets <em>redefinedElement</em> → <em>redefinedClassifier</em>}</td><td>An interface now references all the interfaces that are redefined by this interface.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>SUBSETTING ADDED<br /></strong><strong>Note:</strong> These features were already available in earlier versions of MagicDraw.</td></tr><tr><td>Duration::expr:ValueSpecification[0..1] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td><td colspan="1" rowspan="18">Element or elements can be selected or created only under the owned element’s scope.</td></tr><tr><td>LinkEndData::qualifier:QualifierValue[*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>LinkAction::endData:LinkEndData[2..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElemen</em>t}</td></tr><tr><td>CreateLinkAction::endData:LinkEndCreationData[0..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>DestroyLinkAction::endData:LinkEndDestructionData[2..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>TimeExpression::expr:ValueSpecification[0..1] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>ValuePin::value:ValueSpecification[1] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>State::deferrableTrigger:Trigger[0..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>StructuredActivityNode::edge:ActivityEdge[0..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>StructuredActivityNode::node:ActivityNode[0..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>ValueSpecificationAction::value: ValueSpecification[1] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>AcceptEventAction::trigger:Triger[1..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>Stereotype::icon:Image[0..*] {subsets Element::ownedElement}</td></tr><tr><td>TimeEvent::when:TimeExpression[1] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>InteractionUse::argument:ValueSpecification[*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>StructuredActivityNode::node:ActivityNode [0..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>SequenceNode::executableNode: ExecutableNode[0..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>Transition::trigger:Trigger[0..*] {subsets <em>Element::ownedElement</em>}</td></tr><tr><td>Classifier::/feature {subsets <em>Namespace::member</em>}</td><td>The /feature property of Classifier is now included as a part of the member property.</td></tr><tr><td>Feature::/featuringClassifier {subsets <em>NamedElement::memberNamespace</em>}</td><td>The /featuringClassifier property of Feature is now included as a part of the memberNamespace property.</td></tr><tr><td>Property::owningAssociation {subsets <em>RedefinableElement::redefinitionContext</em>}</td><td>A property now references the owning association of this property.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>DEFAULT VALUE CHANGES</strong></td></tr><tr><td>DurationObservation::firstEvent:Boolean[0..2] <span>=True</span></td><td colspan="1" rowspan="2">The default value has been removed. In effect, the value of the firstEvent property is now by default empty.</td></tr><tr><td>DurationConstraint::firstEvent:Boolean[0..2] <span>=True</span></td></tr><tr><td>PackageableElement::visibility:VisibilityKind[1] =False → Public</td><td>The default value of this property has been changed to Public.This means that packageable elements are now by default public.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>MULTIPLICITY CHANGES</strong></td></tr><tr><td>EnumerationLiteral::enumeration:Enumeration [0..1] → [1]</td><td>The Enumeration must now be specified. It has become mandatory.</td></tr><tr><td>Multiplicity of ExecutionSpecification association with ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification: [1] → [0..2]</td><td>ExecutionSpecification is now associated with two ExecutionOccurrenceSpecifications:<br /><ul><li>The start ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification that designates the start of an action or behavior.</li><li>The finish ExecutionOccurrenceSpecification that designates the finish of an action or behavior.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td colspan="2" rowspan="1"><strong>DERIVATION CHANGES</strong></td></tr><tr><td>Constraint::/context → context</td><td>The context property of the constraint is no longer derived. It is now a settable property.</td></tr><tr><td>Message::/signature → signature</td><td>The signature of the message is no longer derived. However, it remains read-only.</td></tr></tbody></table><h4 class="bold">Notation changes</h4><ol><li><p>If there is a value defined for the URI property of a package, model, or profile, it is automatically displayed on the corresponding shape. The following figure depicts an example of the package notation with the URI property value defined:<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="package_uri_notation.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported UML Specification Changes from Version 2.3 to 2.4.1" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li><p>If the isID property is set to true for the selected attribute, the {id} modifier is displayed in the property modifiers group on the class shape. The following figure depicts an example of the attribute notation when the isID property is set to true:<br /><br /><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="131"><ri:attachment ri:filename="isID_property_notation.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported UML Specification Changes from Version 2.3 to 2.4.1" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li><p>The name of a synchronous message is now displayed on a diagram pane, even if the message does not have a signal or an operation assigned. The following figure depicts an example of the synchronous message name notation:<br /><br /><ac:image ac:height="150"><ri:attachment ri:filename="synchCall_message_name.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported UML Specification Changes from Version 2.3 to 2.4.1" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li><p>Brackets “()” are no more added to a call message name if the message does not have a signal or an operation assigned. The following figure depicts an example of the call message name notation:<br /><br /><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="153"><ri:attachment ri:filename="call_message_name.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Supported UML Specification Changes from Version 2.3 to 2.4.1" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol>
````

## PAGE 01227: Suspect Links

- page_id: `136712964`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712964/Suspect+Links
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:15:12.052+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

Working on a server project requires up-to-date knowledge of all relevant project changes. Thus, we introduce a new set of validation rules for suspect link analysis. These rules help you track changes in Requirements that are linked to design elements (such as SysML Blocks, Test Cases, Use Cases, etc.) by Satisfy, Derive, Refine or Verify relationships, and stay informed about unlinked and deprecated Requirements in the model.

700500

INLINE

The following topics contain more information on Requirements tracking:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Suspect links tracking options' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Suspect links tracking options' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes' space='MD2024x']

You can visit the page[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Suspect links semantics' space='MD2024x']to learn more about the topic.

You can also create your own suspect links customization to keep track of the changes in the elements of the specified type. To learn more, read the following topic:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing suspect links tracking' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712987 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01228: Suspect links semantics

- page_id: `136712987`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712987/Suspect+links+semantics
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:15:12.530+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Suspect Links
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Enabling suspect links tracking applies the *<<track>>* stereotype to the relationships. It is applied to the relationships automatically right after enabling the suspect links tracking, changing the tracking scope, or creating a new relationship (in the case when suspect links are already enabled) if the following criteria are satisfied:

- The relationship type is Satisfy, Verify, Refine, or deriveReq. If custom suspect links tracking is created, the relationship type, direction, source, and target element types should be as defined in the configuration.
- The relationship itself and the relationship's source and target elements are under the specified tracking scope.

The stereotype contains *fingerprint* and *version* tags:

- fingerprint: is used to indicate that changes occurred at the end of a relationship. In other words, the fingerprint is used to highlight that relationship is suspected.
- version: is used to get the Teamwork Cloud server project version, which should be compared to the latest in order to identify what changes occurred in the linked element . In other words, the version is used when the action "Show Changes" is performed on a suspected link.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Upon enabling suspect links tracking, the stereotype *<<track>>*, which contains fingerprint and version tags, is applied tothe relationship 'Satisfy'.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Enabling suspect links tracking applies the <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">&lt;&lt;track&gt;&gt;</em> stereotype to the relationships. It is applied to the relationships automatically right after enabling the suspect links tracking, changing the tracking scope, or creating a new relationship (in the case when suspect links are already enabled) if the following criteria are satisfied:</p><ul><li>The relationship type is Satisfy, Verify, Refine, or deriveReq. If custom suspect links tracking is created, the relationship type, direction, source, and target element types should be as defined in the configuration.</li><li>The relationship itself and the relationship's source and target elements are under the specified tracking scope.</li></ul><p>The stereotype contains <em>fingerprint</em> and <em>version</em> tags:</p><ul><li>fingerprint: is used to indicate<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> that changes occurred at the end of a relationship. In other words, the fingerprint is used to highlight that relationship is suspected.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">version: </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">is used to get the Teamwork Cloud server project version, which should be compared to the latest in order to identify what changes occurred in the linked element</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">. <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In other words, t</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">he version is used when the action &quot;Show Changes&quot; is performed on a suspected link.</span></span></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-bottom: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="suspect_links_semantics.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Suspect links semantics" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br />Upon enabling suspect links tracking, the stereotype <em>&lt;&lt;track&gt;&gt;</em>, which contains fingerprint and version tags, <span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">is applied to </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">the relationship 'Satisfy'</span>.</h6><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712979 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01229: Suspect links tracking options

- page_id: `136712979`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712979/Suspect+links+tracking+options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:15:12.244+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Suspect Links
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

767157416

767157437

767157414

**On this page**

**3**

767157427

##### Tracking Deprecated Requirements

Suspect links analysis allows tracking deprecated Requirements. This is useful when you are working with Requirements and some of them are no longer used. In that case, apply the *«deprecated»* stereotype to mark the Requirements of this type. All deprecated Requirements are provided in the **Validation Results** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To check whether deprecated Requirements are tracked

- In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options . If the Track Deprecated Elements option is set to true , tracking deprecated elements is enabled. [ATTACHMENT filename='track_deprecated_elements_option.png']

##### Tracking Unlinked Requirements

Suspect links analysis allows tracking unlinked Requirements. This is useful when you're working with structure elements and you need to know which Requirements remain unsatisfied.

To check whether unlinked Requirements are satisfied

1. In the main toolbar, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options .
2. Click the arrow next to the Track Unlinked Requirements option and choose: a) All – if you want to track all the unlinked Requirements b) Excluding Owning – if you want to track only those unlinked Requirements, that have no sub-Requirements c) None – if you want to disable tracking unlinked Requirements. [ATTACHMENT filename='excluding_owning.png']
3. Click OK .

All unlinked Requirements are provided in the **Validation Results** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Tracking Links in Main Project Only

You can limit the suspect links analysis scope to links contained in the main project only. If the option is enabled, used projects are excluded from the tracking scope.

To track links in the main project only

- In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options . If the Track Links in Main Project Only option is set to true , links are tracked in the main project only. [ATTACHMENT filename='track_links_in_main_project_only.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

This is useful when you are working with Requirements and some of them are no longer used. In that case, apply the <em>«deprecated»</em> stereotype to mark the Requirements of this type. All deprecated Requirements are provided in the <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Validation Results</strong> panel.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="deprecated_requirements_validation_results_panel.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Suspect links tracking options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </p><p><br /></p><p>To check whether deprecated Requirements are tracked</p><hr /><ul><li>In the main menu, go to <strong>Analyze</strong> &gt; <strong>Suspect Links</strong> and select <strong>Options</strong>. If the <strong>Track Deprecated Elements</strong> option is set to <em>true</em>, tracking deprecated elements is enabled.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="track_deprecated_elements_option.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Suspect links tracking options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><h3>Tracking Unlinked Requirements</h3><p>Suspect links analysis allows tracking unlinked Requirements. This is useful when you're working with structure elements and you need to know which Requirements remain unsatisfied.</p><p><br /></p><p>To check whether unlinked Requirements are satisfied</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main toolbar, go to <strong>Analyze</strong> &gt; <strong>Suspect Links</strong> and select <strong>Options</strong>.</li><li>Click the arrow next to the <strong>Track Unlinked Requirements</strong> option and choose:<br />a) <strong>All </strong>– if you want to track all the unlinked Requirements<br />b) <strong>Excluding Owning</strong> – if you want to track only those unlinked Requirements, that have no sub-Requirements<br />c) <strong>None</strong> – if you want to disable tracking unlinked Requirements.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="excluding_owning.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Suspect links tracking options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /></li></ol><p>All unlinked Requirements are provided in the <strong>Validation Results</strong> panel.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="unlinked_requirements_validation_results_panel.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Suspect links tracking options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Tracking Links in Main Project Only</h3><p>You can limit the suspect links analysis scope to links contained in the main project only. If the option is enabled, used projects are excluded from the tracking scope. </p><p>To track links in the main project only</p><hr /><ul><li>In the main menu, go to <strong>Analyze</strong> &gt; <strong>Suspect Links</strong> and select <strong>Options</strong>. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">If the </span><strong>Track Links in Main Project Only </strong>option <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">is set to </span><em>true</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">, links are tracked in the main project only.</span><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="track_links_in_main_project_only.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Suspect links tracking options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 01230: Swapping

- page_id: `136711589`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711589/Swapping
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:46.384+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Refactoring
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Swapping allows counter-change symbols with each other on the diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can swap the symbols in the following diagrams:

- In the Activity diagram, you can swap actions, merge or decision nodes, join or fork nodes with each other, if the symbols are connected through control flow.
- In the State diagram, you can swap states or pseudo states (initial state, deep history, join, fork, junction, choice, entry point, etc.) with each other, if symbols are connected through transition.

To swap elements, do one of the following

- Drag one shape onto another. Swap does not work when dragged shape can be added into the target shape (or in other words, can be added as a child).
- On the diagram, select the two shapes that you want to swap, right-click, and from the shortcut menu, select Refactor > Swap .

- Swapping does not work when symbols have different parents.
- If you cannot swap within a Teamwork Cloud project, make sure you have the right to edit the model of this project and then try to lock for edit this diagram.

In the following example, you can see the cases when you can swap the elements and the cases when you cannot swap the elements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

| Rule | Example |
| --- | --- |
| You can swap the elements that are in the same branch | You can swap the following elements:“Select the reading item to remove” with “Confirm deletion”“Request removal confirmation” with “Check if there are loaned copies of reading item”“Check if there are loaned copies of reading item” with “Notify librarian and do not allow removal”“Check if there are loaned copies of reading item” with “Check if reading item is reserved” |
| You cannot swap the elements that have different parents | You cannot swap the following elements:“Select the reading item to remove” with Request removal confirmation, because one element is in the “Librarian” partition and the other is in the “System” partition.“Confirm deletion” with “Check if there are loaned copies of reading item” because one element is in the “Librarian” partition and the other is in the “System” partition. |
| You cannot swap the elements that are in different branches | You cannot swap the following elements:“Notify librarian and do not allow removal” with “Check if reading item is reserved”. |

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Refactoring' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711364 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01231: Swimlanes

- page_id: `136711364`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711364/Swimlanes
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T10:20:45.258+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1404690412

1404690415

1404690414

INLINE

Actions and subactivities can be organized into Swimlanes in the Activity diagrams. Swimlanes are used to organize responsibility for actions and subactivities according to class. They often correspond to the organizational units in a business model.

Swimlanes limit and provide a view of the behaviors invoked in the activities. They consist of one or more partitions and can be vertical or horizontal.

An Activity diagram can be visually divided into Swimlanes, each separated from the neighboring Swimlanes by vertical or horizontal solid lines on both sides. Each Swimlane represents a responsibility for part of the overall activity, and may eventually be implemented by one or more objects. The relative ordering of the Swimlanes has no semantic significance, but can indicate some affinity. Each action is assigned to one Swimlane. Transitions can cross lanes. The routing of a transition path is non-essential.

You can customize Swimlane representation by changing its symbol property values. Press Alt + Enter to open the appropriate dialog. Each property is described in the description area on this dialog.

To create a Swimlane

1. On the Activity diagram palette, click the **Vertical Swimlanes** button [IMAGE alt='' src='']and then click an empty space on the diagram pane.
2. If the **Represent Properties** dialog opens, select the properties you want to display as Swimlanes in the diagram (see the following figure) and click **OK**. Otherwise, a few empty Swimlanes are created. The **Represent Properties** dialog opens if:The Activity diagram is owned by a classifier, such as a Class, Component, Use Case.The classifier or its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying a Use Case subject' space=''] has properties.Partitions are created for each property you select to represent as a Swimlane in the Activity diagram. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can also create a Swimlane by dragging one or more properties, Actors, Classes, or Instance Specifications from the Containment tree to the Activity diagram pane. Partitions for each element are created as well.

To set representative elements, do one of the following

- Open the Specification window of the Swimlane and click the cell of the Represents property value. Then click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dot_button.png'] and select the element. Click OK when you are done.
- From the Containment tree, drag the representative element to the partition on the diagram.

To add an additional partition, do one of the following

- On the diagram, right-click the Swimlane, select Insert Swimlane and then Insert Vertical Swimlane or Insert Horizontal Swimlane .
- From the Containment tree, drag one or more property, actor, class or instance specification elements to the Swimlane on the diagram.

To draw multidimensional Swimlanes

1. Draw a vertical Swimlane.
2. From the Swimlane shortcut menu, select Insert Horizontal Swimlane .
3. Insert as many horizontal and vertical Swimlanes as you need.

To add model elements to a Swimlane

- If a Swimlane is already drawn in the Activity diagram, drawing an action (or any other element) highlights the Swimlane in blue. This means the action shape depends on the Swimlane symbol. If the model elements depend on a Swimlane symbol, they are deleted if the Swimlane symbol is deleted. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To rearrange Swimlanes, do one of the following

- Drag any of the Swimlanes in the direction where you want it to be.
- Click the heading of any Swimlane and use the displayed control arrow to move the Swimlane left, right, up or down. [ATTACHMENT filename='move_swimlane_left_button.png']

To delete a single Swimlane entirely, do one of the following

- Click the header of the Swimlane, then press Delete.
- Click the header of the Swimlane, then press Ctrl + D.
- Right-click the header or any empty space on the Swimlane, then select Delete Swimlane .

To delete all Swimlanes and their contents

- Click any empty space on the Swimlanes, then press Ctrl + D.

To delete Swimlanes without deleting contained elements, do one of the following

- Click any empty space on the Swimlanes, then press Delete.
- Right-click the headers or anywhere on the Swimlanes, then select Delete All Swimlanes, Keep Contents .

1404690411
