<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714448 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01256: Text tool

- page_id: `136714448`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714448/Text+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:36.875+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

62959207

62959209

62959208

A Text tool provides extra functions to convert or format text such as change text to bold, italics, add an underline, equals sign etc. The following figure shows a class diagram to represent the TextTool API.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Class Diagram: TextTool.

Like other Custom tools, the Text tool (*text.jar*) must be installed in the 'extensions' folder of the Report Wizard plugin. For further information on Custom tools and installation, see *[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom tool' space='MD2024x']*.

To use the APIs of Texttool or other custom tools

1. Place the jar file in the extensions folder of the Report Wizard plugin folder.
2. Start MagicDraw.
3. Import it to your report by typing the following:

```text

```

62959206

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e3596ca-88a4-48db-8dbf-4ab2c399d664"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">62959207</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6253de1a-1045-434d-bfdf-7895b4162c8c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">62959209</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09c380f0-417c-47e2-86fd-fa640aa01425"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">62959208</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A Text tool provides <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c9ea2912-8208-4c80-9629-e42bdb9ef18a">extra functions</ac:inline-comment-marker> to convert or format text such as change text to bold, italics, add an underline, equals sign etc. The following figure shows a class diagram to represent the TextTool API.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="A Class Diagram - TextTool.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Text tool" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Class Diagram: <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9f66118b-0c2b-45e3-b514-6ec606b0f054">TextTool</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</h6><p>Like other Custom tools, the Text tool (<em>text.jar</em>) must be installed in the 'extensions' folder of the Report Wizard plugin. For further information on Custom tools and installation, see <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom tool" /></ac:link></em>.</p><p>To use the APIs of Texttool or other custom tools</p><hr /><ol><li>Place the jar file in the <strong>extensions</strong> folder of the Report Wizard plugin folder.</li><li>Start MagicDraw.</li><li>Import it to your report by typing the following:</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5a07ad1d-c5b2-4c95-a508-79b937338cad"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import('text', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.TextTool')]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="85f87ca4-3ee7-4584-a2e6-2783d2e9b333"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">62959206</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="70c17d73-1a9a-4ae5-b958-a8cb3099265a" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714452 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01257: Text tool API

- page_id: `136714452`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714452/Text+tool+API
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:36.982+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Text tool
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: An example of the getPureText(String HTMLString) method

63861144

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: An example of the getPureText(String HTMLString) method

63861146

63861141

**On this page**

44

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: An example of the getPureText(String HTMLString) method

63861145

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Class diagram: TextTool

###### noLineBreak(String text)

This method removes all control characters such as U+0009 (Tab), U+000A (Line Feed), and U+000D (Carriage Return) from a given text. This method also filters text under the category "Cc" in the Unicode specification.

#### INFO: An example of a use case that contains multiple lines

false

An example of a use case that contains multiple lines

**Code line:**

```text
$usecase.name
```

**The output:**

Line 1

Line 2

Line 3

#### INFO: An example of the $text.noLineBreak() method

false

An example of the $text.noLineBreak() method

**Code line:**

```text
$text.noLineBreak($usecase.name)
```

**The output:**

```text
Line 1 Line 2 Line 3
```

###### toInteger(String text)

This method converts a string argument to a signed decimal integer.

#### INFO: An example of the toInteger(String text) method

false

An example of the toInteger(String text) method

**Code line:**

```text
#set ($int = $text.toInteger('2'))
```

```text
#set ($result = $int + 1)
```

```text
Result is $result
```

###### toDouble(String text)

This method converts a string argument to a signed decimal double.

****

#### INFO: An example of the toDouble(String text) method

false

An example of the toDouble(String text) method

**Code line:**

```text
#set ($d = $text.toDouble('10.1'))
```

```text
#set ($result = $d + 2)
```

```text
Result is $result
```

###### italic(String text)

This method forces a report to render a given text in the italic style.

#### INFO: An example of the italic(String text) method

false

An example of the italic(String text) method

**Code line:**

```text
The $text.italic($class.name) must provide interface for web services.
```

**The output:**

The*BusinessServices*must provide interface for web services.

###### bold(String text)

This method forces a report to render a given text in the bold style.

#### INFO: An example of the bold(String text) method

false

An example of the bold(String text) method

**Code line:**

```text
Method $text.bold($operator.name) must be implemented by implementation class.
```

**The output:**

Method**doService**must be implemented by implementation class.

###### underline(String text)

This method forces a report to render a given text in the underlined style.

#### INFO: An example of the underline(String text) method

false

An example of the underline(String text) method

**Code line:**

```text
Attribute $text.underline ($attribute.name) contains a service name.
```

**The output:**

AttributeserverNamecontains a service name.

###### html(String text)

This method forces a report to render a given text as HTML.

#### INFO: An example of the html(String text) method

false

An example of the html(String text) method

**Code line:**

```text
$text.html('<ul><li>A</li><li>B</li></ul>')
```

**The output:**

- A
- B

For more information on supported *HTML* tags, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Appendix D: HTML tag support' space='MD'].

****

###### getString(String text)

This methods converts text from *RTF* to a Java String.

#### INFO: An example of the getString(String text) method

false

An example of the getString(String text) method

**Code line:**

```text
#set ($str = $text.getString("Ubersetzer"))
```

**The output:**

Ubersetzer

###### equals(String str1, String str2)

This method compares two strings. The result will be true if and only if str1 represents the same sequence of characters as str2 does. This method supports *RTF* text comparison.

#### INFO: An example of the equals(String str1, String str2) method

false

An example of the equals(String str1, String str2) method

**Code line:**

```text
#set ($str = $text.getString("Ubersetzer"))
```

```text
#if ($text.equals($str, "Ubersetzer"))
```

```text
true
```

```text
#end
```

###### getPureText(String HTMLString)

This method gets plain text from an HTML documentation. All HTML tags that are applied to the text will be removed from the text. Only plain text would be returned.

#### INFO: An example of the getPureText(String HTMLString) method

false

An example of the getPureText(String HTMLString) method

**Code line:**

```text
#set ($html = '<html><body><b>Text</b></body></html>')
	$text.getPureText($html)
```

## PAGE 01258: The formal gate and actual gate usage in the sequence diagram

- page_id: `136711044`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711044/The+formal+gate+and+actual+gate+usage+in+the+sequence+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:17.936+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Gate
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

See the following figure where the *getBalance* message is drawn from the diagram frame to the *theirBank* lifeline. The *getBalance* message has a gate.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

See the following figure where the actual gate is presented. The Balance Lookup interaction use refers to the Balance Lookup sequence diagram. The *getBalance* message (see the 2nd message) has selected the formal gate and automatically repeats the data of the *getBalance* message from the Balance Lookup diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714795 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01259: The supported HTML lists

- page_id: `136714795`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714795/The+supported+HTML+lists
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:49.198+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The DocBook engine of Report Wizard supports the following HTML lists:

1. Unordered Lists
2. Ordered Lists
3. Definition Lists

#### Unordered Lists

An unordered list is defined by the <ul> element. An unordered list element <ul> contains one or more <li> elements that define the actual items of the list in bullets. The Unordered List element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Ordered Lists

An ordered list is defined by the <ol> element. The ordered list element <ol> contains one or more <li> elements that define the actual items of the list preceded by numbers. The unordered list element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Definition Lists

A definition list is defined by <dl> element. A definition list element <dl> consists of two parts: (i) A term that is created using the <dt> element and (ii) a description that is created using the <dd> element. The definition list supports one to multiple terms and the corresponding definition(s). The term comes before the definition. 
The definition list element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The DocBook engine of Report Wizard supports the following HTML lists:</p><ol><li>Unordered Lists</li><li>Ordered Lists</li><li>Definition Lists</li></ol><h2>Unordered Lists</h2><p>An unordered list is defined by the &lt;ul&gt; element. An unordered list element &lt;ul&gt; contains one or more &lt;li&gt; elements that define the actual items of the list in bullets. The Unordered List element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Unordered List" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Unordered List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of unordered list.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Unordered List" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Unordered List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of unordered list.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Ordered Lists</h2><p>An ordered list is defined by the &lt;ol&gt; element. The ordered list element &lt;ol&gt; contains one or more &lt;li&gt; elements that define the actual items of the list preceded by numbers. The unordered list element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Ordered List" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Ordered List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of ordered list.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Ordered List" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Ordered List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of ordered list.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Definition Lists</h2><p>A definition list is defined by &lt;dl&gt; element. A definition list element &lt;dl&gt; consists of two parts: (i) A term that is created using the &lt;dt&gt; element and (ii) a description that is created using the &lt;dd&gt; element. The definition list supports one to multiple terms and the corresponding definition(s). The term comes before the definition. <br />The definition list element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of definition list.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Definitions List"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of definition list.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML lists" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714802 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01260: The supported HTML table elements

- page_id: `136714802`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714802/The+supported+HTML+table+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:49.498+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The HTML table element (<table>) is divided into rows (<tr>), and each row may contain a number of table data cells (<td>) or (<th>).

The <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th> elements support two attributes: (i) align and (ii) valign.

**(i) align**

You use align to horizontally arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:

- "left" to left-align the content in a cell.
- "center" to center-align the content in a cell.
- “right” to right-align the content in a cell.
- “justify” to stretch the content with equal width.
- "char" to align the content to a specific character.

**(ii) valign**

You use valign to vertically arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:

- "top" to top-align the content in a cell.
- "middle" to center-align the content in a cell.
- "bottom" to bottom-align the content in a cell.

The table element shown in the figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The HTML table element (&lt;table&gt;) is divided into rows (&lt;tr&gt;), and each row may contain a number of table data cells (&lt;td&gt;) or (&lt;th&gt;).</p><p>The &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt; elements support two attributes: (i) align and (ii) valign.</p><p><strong>(i) align</strong></p><p>You use align to horizontally arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:</p><ul><li>&quot;left&quot; to left-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;center&quot; to center-align the content in a cell.</li><li>“right” to right-align the content in a cell.</li><li>“justify” to stretch the content with equal width.</li><li>&quot;char&quot; to align the content to a specific character.</li></ul><p><strong>(ii) valign</strong></p><p>You use valign to vertically arrange items within a table cell. The possible values are as follows:</p><ul><li>&quot;top&quot; to top-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;middle&quot; to center-align the content in a cell.</li><li>&quot;bottom&quot; to bottom-align the content in a cell.</li></ul><p>The table element shown in the figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Table" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML table elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Table" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Table"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported HTML table elements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714760 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01261: The Supported HTML Tags

- page_id: `136714760`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714760/The+Supported+HTML+Tags
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:48.233+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### HTML Paragraph Elements

The HTML tags in the Report Wizard’s engine for DocBook defines how a paragraph is arranged in a DocBook document. There are two categories of paragraph elements:

1.1.1 Paragraph 
1.1.2 Preformatted Text

##### Paragraph

The Paragraph element (<p>) defines a paragraph. The <p> element shown in the first figure will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Preformatted Text

The Preformatted text element (<pre>) displays the text exactly as you expected it to. The <pre> tag preserves both spaces and line breaks. The <pre> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Font Styles

Report Wizard specifies a group of HTML tags that you can use to define how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called font style elements.

1. Teletype Text
2. Italics
3. Bold
4. Strikethrough
5. Underline

##### Teletype Text

The HTML Teletype Text element (<tt>) creates teletype or monospaced text. The <tt> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Italics

The HTML Italics element (<i>) renders text slanted to the right. The <i> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Bold

The HTML Bold element (<b>) renders bold text. The <b> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Strikethrough

The HTML Strikethrough element (<strike> or <s>) renders text with a horizontal strike or a line through the middle. The <strike> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Underline

The HTML Underline element (<u>) underlines text. The underline <u> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook as in second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Phrase Elements

There is a group of HTML tags that allows you to specify how fonts will be displayed on rendered text. They are called HTML Phrase Tags or Elements. The following is a list of these font style elements:

1. Emphasis
2. Strong
3. Citation
4. Definition
5. A Fragment of Computer Code
6. Sample Text
7. Keyboard Input
8. Variable

##### Emphasis

The Emphasis element (<em>) emphasizes text content and displays it in italics. The <em> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Strong

The Strong element (<strong>) strongly emphasizes text content. It renders the text in bold. The <strong> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Citation

The Citation element (<cite>) presents a citation or a reference to other sources. The <cite> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Definition

The HTML Definition element (<dfn>) gives a definition for the term being defined. The Definition element <dfn> shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### A Fragment of Computer Code

The HTML fragment of code element (<code>) designates part of computer code. The <code> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Sample Text

The HTML Sample Text element (<samp>) designates sample output of programs or scripts. The <samp> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Keyboard Input

The HTML Keyboard Input element (<kbd>) indicates user input or text to be entered by the user. The <kbd> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Variable

The HTML Variable element (<var>) indicates an instance of a variable or program argument. The <var> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Link Element

The HTML Link element (<a>) defines a hyperlink that links one page to another. The <a> element supports the attribute href, which specifies the URL of a page the hyperlink goes to.

The <a> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### HTML Image Element

The HTML Image element (<img>) defines an image in an HTML page. The <img> element supports the attribute src, which specifies the URL of an image. 
The <img> element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The &lt;var&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Variable" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Variable"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of variable.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Variable" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Variable"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of variable.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Link Element</h2><p>The HTML Link element (&lt;a&gt;) defines a hyperlink that links one page to another. The &lt;a&gt; element supports the attribute href, which specifies the URL of a page the hyperlink goes to.</p><p>The &lt;a&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Link" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Link"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of link.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Link" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Link"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of link.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>HTML Image Element</h2><p>The HTML Image element (&lt;img&gt;) defines an image in an HTML page. The &lt;img&gt; element supports the attribute src, which specifies the URL of an image.<br />The &lt;img&gt; element shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Image" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of image.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Image" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of image.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The Supported HTML Tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714805 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01262: The supported style sheet properties

- page_id: `136714805`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714805/The+supported+style+sheet+properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:49.731+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix E: DocBook Support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The DocBook engine of Report Wizard supports specific style sheets within an HTML element. The supported style sheet properties are as follows:

1. Font
2. Text Align
3. Text Decoration
4. Vertical Align

#### Font

The “font” property is a shorthand property for setting the “font-style”, and “font-weight” properties.

##### Font Style

The “font-style” property defines that the font be displayed either in normal way or in italics within a specified font family. The font-style shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Font Weight

The “font-weight” property specifies the weight of the font. The values “100” to “900” form an ordered sequence where each value indicates the font weight. The keyword ‘normal’ is synonymous with “400”, and “bold” is synonymous with “700”.

The following values are defined:

- normal, lighter, 100, 200, 300, and 400 will be rendered as ‘normal’.
- bold, bolder, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 will be rendered as ‘bold’.

The font-weight property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Text Align

The text-align property specifies the horizontal alignment of inline content of a block. The supported tags of the text-align property are <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th>. The values of this property are:

- "left" to left-align the content.
- "center" to center-align the content.
- “right” to right-align the content.
- “justify” to stretch the content with equal width.
- "char" to align the content to a specific character.

The text-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Text Decoration

The text-decoration property specifies decorations that are added to text. The values of this property are none, underline, or line-through. The text-decoration property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Vertical Align

The vertical-align property specifies the vertical alignment of text or an element within a lined box. The supported tags of vertical-align are <table>, <tr>, <td>, and <th>. The values of the vertical-align property are top, middle, and bottom.

- "top" to top-align the content.
- "middle" to center-align the content.
- "bottom" to bottom-align the content.

The vertical-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The DocBook engine of Report Wizard supports specific style sheets within an HTML element. The supported style sheet properties are as follows:</p><ol><li>Font</li><li>Text Align</li><li>Text Decoration</li><li>Vertical Align</li></ol><h2>Font</h2><p>The “font” property is a shorthand property for setting the “font-style”, and “font-weight” properties.</p><h3>Font Style</h3><p>The “font-style” property defines that the font be displayed either in normal way or in italics within a specified font family. The font-style shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Font Style" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Font Style"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of font style.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Style" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Style"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of font style.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Font Weight</h3><p>The “font-weight” property specifies the weight of the font. The values “100” to “900” form an ordered sequence where each value indicates the font weight. The keyword ‘normal’ is synonymous with “400”, and “bold” is synonymous with “700”.</p><p>The following values are defined:</p><ul><li>normal, lighter, 100, 200, 300, and 400 will be rendered as ‘normal’.</li><li>bold, bolder, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 will be rendered as ‘bold’.</li></ul><p>The font-weight property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of font weight.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Weight" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Font Weight"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of font weight.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Text Align</h2><p>The text-align property specifies the horizontal alignment of inline content of a block. The supported tags of the text-align property are &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt;. The values of this property are:</p><ul><li>&quot;left&quot; to left-align the content.</li><li>&quot;center&quot; to center-align the content.</li><li>“right” to right-align the content.</li><li>“justify” to stretch the content with equal width.</li><li>&quot;char&quot; to align the content to a specific character.</li></ul><p>The text-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Align" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of text align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Align" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of text align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Text Decoration</h2><p>The text-decoration property specifies decorations that are added to text. The values of this property are none, underline, or line-through. The text-decoration property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of text decoration.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Text Decoration"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of text decoration.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Vertical Align</h2><p>The vertical-align property specifies the vertical alignment of text or an element within a lined box. The supported tags of vertical-align are &lt;table&gt;, &lt;tr&gt;, &lt;td&gt;, and &lt;th&gt;. The values of the vertical-align property are top, middle, and bottom.</p><ul><li>&quot;top&quot; to top-align the content.</li><li>&quot;middle&quot; to center-align the content.</li><li>&quot;bottom&quot; to bottom-align the content.</li></ul><p>The vertical-align property shown in the first figure below will be rendered in a DocBook format as in the second figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align" ac:alt="HTML Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="HTML tag for fragments of vertical align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align" ac:alt="DocBook Tag for a Fragment of Vertical Align"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DocBook tag for fragments of vertical align.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="The supported style sheet properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

## PAGE 01263: The Use Case scenario created with version 17.0.1 or earlier

- page_id: `136711439`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711439/The+Use+Case+scenario+created+with+version+17.0.1+or+earlier
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:40.280+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Use Case > Use Case Scenario sketch
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

880408184

880408186

880408185

Beginning with version 17.0.2 of MagicDraw or other modeling tools developed by us, Use Case scenarios of projects created with earlier versions are stored in the **Use Case Scenario Obsolete**property group in the Use Case [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- To see the Use Case Scenario Obsolete property group, change the property display mode to Expert .
- The Use Case Scenario Obsolete property group is displayed only if the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case description profile' space='MD2024x'] is loaded.

880408183

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715578 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01264: Thrown exception tag value translation

- page_id: `136715578`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715578/Thrown+exception+tag+value+translation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T13:30:44.196+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering > Conversion from an Earlier Project Version
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1398533760

1398533762

1398533761

The stereotype «C++Operation» can be applied to an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operation' space='MD185'].

In the modeling tool stereotype, «C++Operation» must apply all operations; thedefaultthrow exceptiontag value is*any*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### C++ThrownExceptions tag value

| Old value | Translation |
| --- | --- |
| Operation does not apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in an old profile (C++ Profile). | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the throw exception tag value to any. |
| Operation applies the «C++Operation» stereotype in an old profile (C++ Profile) and set the C++ThrownExceptions tag value to any. | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the throw exception tag value to any. Remove the «C++Operation» stereotype (old profile) and the C++ThrownExceptions tag value. |
| Operation applies the «C++Operation» stereotype in old profile (C++ Profile) and set the C++ThrownExceptions tag value to none. | Apply the «C++Operation» stereotype in a new profile (c++ ANSI profile) and set the throw exception tag value to none. Remove the «C++Operation» stereotype (old profile) and the C++ThrownExceptions tag value. |

1398533758

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conversion from an Earlier Project Version' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='']

23459284502

39583750985

INLINE

435982745

INLINE

#### NOTE: Functionalities

Functionalities

This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

A Time Diagram is an extension of UML notation. The time diagram is similar to a sequence diagram, but the model elements of the time diagram have predefined stereotypes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Time diagram

45928452890

INLINE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f4b0c983-1855-4cf0-80aa-7e3c867581c8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">23459284502</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e3448529-1c36-45d9-ad75-4ff074a989ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">39583750985</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba2b2144-a84e-42ad-9c67-907924a03ddf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">435982745</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="da8ad82c-2106-4069-b48f-79ef031e2740"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Functionalities </ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span>A Time Diagram is an extension of UML notation. The time diagram is similar to a sequence diagram, but the model elements of the time diagram have predefined stereotypes.</span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="time_diagram.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Time diagram" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Time diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="563543fa-6ebd-42dd-84fc-ea79976bc4d7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">45928452890</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related Pages:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Extension diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extension diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Diagramming" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714607 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01266: Tool interface

- page_id: `136714607`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714607/Tool+interface
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:43.069+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Custom Tool is written in the Java language. The tool implements a specialized interface called ITool. The Report API provides both an interface and a class to support interface realization and class generalization. As mentioned earlier, a Tool is modeled on the JavaBean specifications. Functions implemented in this class must be defined in a series of setter or getter methods. The following sample shows the source code for the ITool interface that you must implement:

```text

```

All tools must implement the ITool interface (or one of its subclasses) as it defines the methods the template engine calls to execute. The table below provides a description of the methods in the ITool interface.

The table below lists Description of Methods in ITool interface.

| Method | Description |
| --- | --- |
| setContext(Context context) | Once the class has been initialized, this method is called by the template engine runtime to set the template context. Overriding the current context will affect the code after this tool. |
| getContext() | Return the template context that is assigned to the current runtime. |
| setProperties(Properties properties) | This method is called by the template engine runtime, once the class has been initialized, to set the template properties. Overriding the current properties will not affect the engine. |
| getProperties() | Return the current template properties. |
| VOID | Represents the Void class. VOID is used to make sure that returning data from the setter method is absolutely nothing. In general, Velocity considers the return of void from the setter method as a ‘null’ value. This causes the word 'null' to be printed out in the report. To avoid this problem, the setter method may use VOID as a return object. |
| class Void | A void class is used as a return in the Tool when you want to be sure that nothing is returned to the context. |
| class RetainedString | A direct command report formatter to keep the referenced String format. The formatter and other reference insertion handlers should maintain Strings directed by this class. |

The other classes that can be extended are Tool and ConcurrentTool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### ITool Interface And Related Class.

#### Class Tool

A Class Tool is a base class realizing the interface ITool. This class provides the default implementation for ITool.

This class also provides methods derived from the java.util.Observable class, which can be used to notify the observers. An observer class, such as a template engine or a graphical user interface class, can receive the notification message from the tool and manage to display or interact with the user.

#### Concurrent Tool

A Concurrent Tool provides concurrent tasks running in the template engine. This class implements an unbounded thread-safe queue, which arranges the elements in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) order. New tasks are inserted at the tail of the queue, and the queue retrieval operations obtain elements at the head of the queue.

The tool extends from this class and is ideal for processing a long task that does not want other tasks to wait until the process is complete. The following code shows the sample usage of Concurrent Tool.

```text

```

An example of Concurrent Tool is *FileTool*. The root template that calls the **file tool** method will create a subprocess, offer the subprocess into a queue, and continue the root template until finished. The template engine will later call the **consume** method to complete the subprocess once the consumer queue is available. The number of concurrent processes available for the execution is declared in the template engine property (See **TemplateConstants.TEMPLATE_POOL_SIZE** for detail.).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Custom Tool is written in the Java language. The tool implements a specialized interface called ITool. The Report API provides both an interface and a class to support interface realization and class generalization. As mentioned earlier, a Tool is modeled on the JavaBean specifications. Functions implemented in this class must be defined in a series of setter or getter methods. The following sample shows the source code for the ITool interface that you must implement:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3f871750-90ad-43ef-8905-892179d56e4e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public interface ITool extends IBean
{
   // attributes
   Void VOID = new Void();
   // inner classes
   public class Void{}
   public class RetainedString implements CharSequence{}
   // methods 
   void setContext(IContext context);
   String getContext();
   void setProperties(Properties properties);
   Properties getProperties();
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>All tools must implement the ITool interface (or one of its subclasses) as it defines the methods the template engine calls to execute. The table below provides a description of the methods in the ITool interface.</p><p>The table below lists Description of Methods in ITool interface.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Method</th><th> Description</th></tr><tr><td>setContext(Context context)</td><td>Once the class has been initialized, <br />this method is called by the template <br />engine runtime to set the template <br />context. Overriding the current context <br />will affect the code after this tool.</td></tr><tr><td>getContext()</td><td>Return the template context that is <br />assigned to the current runtime.</td></tr><tr><td>setProperties(Properties properties)</td><td>This method is called by the template <br />engine runtime, once the class has <br />been initialized, to set the template <br />properties. Overriding the current <br />properties will not affect the engine.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">getProperties()</td><td colspan="1">Return the current template properties.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">VOID</td><td colspan="1">Represents the Void class. VOID is used <br />to make sure that returning data from the <br />setter method is absolutely nothing. In <br />general, Velocity considers the return of void <br />from the setter method as a ‘null’ value. <br />This causes the word 'null' to be printed <br />out in the report. To avoid this problem, <br />the setter method may use VOID as a return <br />object.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">class Void</td><td colspan="1"><p>A void class is used as a return in the Tool <br />when you want to be sure that nothing is <br />returned to the context.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">class RetainedString</td><td colspan="1">A direct command report formatter <br />to keep the referenced String format. <br />The formatter and other reference insertion <br />handlers should maintain Strings directed by <br />this class.</td></tr></tbody></table><p>The other classes that can be extended are Tool and ConcurrentTool. </p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="ITool Interface And Related Class." ac:alt="ITool Interface And Related Class."><ri:attachment ri:filename="Class Diagram.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Tool interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">ITool Interface And Related Class.</h6><h2>Class Tool</h2><p>A Class Tool is a base class realizing the interface ITool. This class provides the default implementation for ITool.</p><p>This class also provides methods derived from the java.util.Observable class, which can be used to notify the observers. An observer class, such as a template engine or a graphical user interface class, can receive the notification message from the tool and manage to display or interact with the user.</p><h2>Concurrent Tool</h2><p>A Concurrent Tool provides concurrent tasks running in the template engine. This class implements an unbounded thread-safe queue, which arranges the elements in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) order. New tasks are inserted at the tail of the queue, and the queue retrieval operations obtain elements at the head of the queue.</p><p>The tool extends from this class and is ideal for processing a long task that does not want other tasks to wait until the process is complete. The following code shows the sample usage of Concurrent Tool.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b569d5c-816c-43b7-af13-12a47a93233f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.nomagic.magicreport.engine.ConcurrentTool;
public class LongTaskTool extends ConcurrentTool
{
   public String longTask() {
      // enqueue object
      offer(new ConsumeObject(referent));
   }
   public void consume(ConsumeObject consumeObject) {
      Object referent = consumeObject.get();
      // process long task
   }
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>An example of Concurrent Tool is <em>FileTool</em>. The root template that calls the <strong>file tool</strong> method will create a subprocess, offer the subprocess into a queue, and continue the root template until finished. The template engine will later call the <strong>consume</strong> method to complete the subprocess once the consumer queue is available. The number of concurrent processes available for the execution is declared in the template engine property (See <strong>TemplateConstants.TEMPLATE_POOL_SIZE</strong> for detail.).</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709202 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01267: Toolbars

- page_id: `136709202`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709202/Toolbars
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:11:38.003+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > MagicDraw getting started > Understanding the user interface
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Restoring default toolbars

1421810870

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Restoring default toolbars

1421810872

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Restoring default toolbars

1421810871

#### EXCERPT: Restoring default toolbars

INLINE

Toolbars help to speed up your work when performing commonly used tasks. Your modeling tool has two types of toolbars:

- Main toolbars - contain commands for basic project-related tasks and are located at the top of the modeling tool window, right under the main menu.
- Diagram toolbars - contain commands for working with diagram symbols and are located at the top of the diagram pane.

To show or hide different toolbars

- In the main menu, click View , select either Main Toolbars or Diagram Toolbars , and in the displayed menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.
- Right-click the desired toolbar area and in the shortcut menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.

##### Customizing toolbars

You can customize existing toolbars by adding or removing buttons and also create new custom toolbars.

To add a new toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar group and click the Add > New Toolbar .
3. Enter the name of the new toolbar and click OK .

After completing the above steps, a new toolbar is created in the selected toolbar group, and you can choose to show/hide it in the shortcut menu of the respective toolbar area. Note that the newly created toolbar does not have any buttons, so you need to add them after creating it.

To add a new button to a toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar and click the Add > Button . [ATTACHMENT filename='customize_toolbars_dialog.png']
3. In the Add Button dialog, select the desired button or command and click OK .
4. Click OK one more time to close the Customize Toolbars dialog.

A new button is added to the selected toolbar. To add more than one button, repeat steps 2 and 3 as many times as you need.

To remove a toolbar or button

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars / Diagram Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the toolbar or button you want to remove and click the Remove button.

After completing the above steps, the selected toolbar or button is removed from the respective toolbar area or toolbar. Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.

#### NOTE: Restoring default toolbars

Restoring default toolbars

To undo all the actions for customizing toolbars, open the **Customize Toolbars** dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the **Reset to Defaults** button.

Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="67428948-3ddd-4460-9978-eac77bf76b73"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Restoring default toolbars</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To undo all the actions for customizing toolbars, open the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the <strong>Reset to Defaults</strong> button.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="947a133f-8ff7-4609-a416-b63e2de23099"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1421810869</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /></ac:link></li><li class="_mce_tagged_br"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Main menu" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Model Browser" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Image Library tab" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712163 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01269: Traceability

- page_id: `136712163`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712163/Traceability
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T15:00:34.433+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Model Sample

525815827

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Model Sample

525815831

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Model Sample

525815830

#### INFO: Compatibility

Compatibility

The traceability solution is available in Architect and Enterprise editions.

Our modeling tools support the traceability feature that allows you to track, visualize, navigate, and analyze the elements involved in the traceability relations. The traceability relations are used to relate the elements representing the same concept in different UML models at different levels of abstraction or from different viewpoints. Different levels of abstraction may contain, include, or even correspond to different stages of system development process (starting from requirements analysis and finishing with implementation). The higher level of abstraction (e.g., requirements analysis), contains models with specifying elements, and the lower level of abstraction (e.g., implementation) includes models with realizing ones.

The traceability relations help to determine how your requirements or the other model artifacts are satisfied. As they may change, you can use traceability relations to monitor the impact of these changes.

Multiple types of custom and extended UML relationships (e.g., realization, abstraction), tags (e.g., **Alternative Flow of Events Diagrams** ), and properties (e.g., **Owned Behavior** ) are used to represent traceability relations between the specifying and realizing elements for showing traceability from requirements analysis to implementation and deployment models.

**Relation Map and Dependency Matrix**

You can visualize the traceability relations of your project in order to analyze them using the modeling tool's features, such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Relation Map' space='MD2024x'] (for the analysis of traces among multiple levels of abstraction) and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix' space='MD2024x'] (for the analysis of traces between any two levels of abstraction).

**Element’s Specification window, Properties panel, Go To, and Notes**

You may track and navigate the elements, that are directly or indirectly related to a specific element through the traceability properties that will be represented in a special property group of the element’s [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability properties in Properties panel' space='MD2024x'], or on the element’s shortcut menu under the **Go To** menu. The traceability properties can also be visualized on a diagram using the standard modeling tool mechanism for displaying property values in notes.

**Traceability Report**

A Traceability Report is particularly useful when there is a need to visualize and verify that requirement analysis, design, and implementation model elements are all covered in higher or lower levels of abstraction, for example, all use cases should be covered with design classes realizing them.

#### INFO: Model Sample

Model Sample

The model used in all the examples in this section is the **Traceability** sample model that comes with our modeling tool. To open this sample do one of the following:

- On the Welcome screen, select Samples > Product Features > Traceability .
- Go to the folder <MagicDraw installation directory>\samples\product features and open the Traceability.mdzip file.

525815826

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="92bf424b-1621-4e69-8a56-dc631d7179d1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">525815827</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9c1817e6-8acb-4736-9ca9-0c837ca27455"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">525815831</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1d508e90-7ba4-411e-b064-cf7ca6ec9105"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">525815830</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><br /></p><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8f59b00d-4a98-40a7-a839-0ba09ab5bc64"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Compatibility</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The traceability solution is available in Architect and Enterprise editions.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Our modeling tools support the traceability feature that allows you to track, visualize, navigate, and analyze the elements involved in the traceability relations. The traceability relations are used to relate the elements representing the same concept in different UML models at different levels of abstraction or from different viewpoints. Different levels of abstraction may contain, include, or even correspond to different stages of system development process (starting from requirements analysis and finishing with implementation). The higher level of abstraction (e.g., requirements analysis), contains models with specifying elements, and the lower level of abstraction (e.g., implementation) includes models with realizing ones. </p><p><span>The traceability relations help to determine how your requirements or the other model artifacts are satisfied. As they may change, you can use traceability relations to monitor the impact of these changes. </span></p><p><span>Multiple types of custom and extended UML relationships (e.g., realization, abstraction), tags (e.g., </span> <strong>Alternative Flow of Events Diagrams</strong> <span>), and properties (e.g., </span> <strong>Owned Behavior</strong> <span>) are used to represent traceability relations between the specifying and realizing elements for showing traceability from requirements analysis to implementation and deployment models. </span></p><p><strong>Relation Map and Dependency Matrix </strong></p><p><span>You can visualize the traceability relations of your project in order to analyze them using the modeling tool's features, such as <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Relation Map" /></ac:link> (for the analysis of traces among multiple levels of abstraction) and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency Matrix" /></ac:link> (for the analysis of traces between any two levels of abstraction). </span></p><p><strong>Element’s Specification window, Properties panel, Go To, and Notes </strong></p><p><span>You may track and navigate the elements, that are directly or indirectly related to a specific element through the traceability properties that will be represented in a special property group of the element’s <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> </span>and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in Properties panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Properties panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, or on the element’s shortcut menu under the <strong>Go To</strong> menu. The traceability properties can also be visualized on a diagram using the standard modeling tool mechanism for displaying property values in notes.</p><p><strong>Traceability Report</strong></p><p>A Traceability Report is particularly useful when there is a need to visualize and verify that requirement analysis, design, and implementation model elements are all covered in higher or lower levels of abstraction, for example, all use cases should be covered with design classes realizing them.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="35369f24-024f-43e8-8ac7-189c34275092"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Model Sample</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="472ddac2-ca79-4b5b-8fd4-b3376fcab4e6">The model used in all the examples in this section is the <strong>Traceability</strong> sample model that comes with our modeling tool. To open this sample do one of the following: </ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ul><li>On the Welcome screen, select <strong>Samples &gt; Product Features &gt; Traceability</strong>. </li><li>Go to the folder <em>&lt;MagicDraw installation directory&gt;\samples\product features </em>and open the<em> Traceability.mdzip</em> file. </li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p></div></div><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94a3647f-b43b-4f08-96da-e2a08cd56fd6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">525815826</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="68a85eee-e515-41db-9f70-99f457e26b71" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712220 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01270: Traceability properties in Go To submenu

- page_id: `136712220`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712220/Traceability+properties+in+Go+To+submenu
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:18.426+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Traceability relations representation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The submenu of the Go To menu on the element’s shortcut menu allows you to easily find and navigate to therelated elements through the traceability relations.

To select an element from the higher/lower level of abstraction in the Containment tree

1. Select the element in the Containment tree or its shape on the diagram.
2. From the element’s shortcut menu, select Go To > Traceability > Specification/ Realization/ Other , choose a property, and then select an element.
3. The element will be selected in the Containment tree.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Traceability properties in the Go To submenu

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<div class="column"><p><span>The submenu of the </span> <span>Go To </span> <span>menu on the element’s shortcut menu allows you to easily find and navigate to the </span>related elements through the traceability relations.</p><p><span>To select an element from the higher/lower level of abstraction in the Containment tree</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Select the element in the Containment tree or its shape on the diagram.</li><li><span>From the element’s shortcut menu, select </span> <span>Go To </span> <span>&gt; </span> <span>Traceability </span> <span>&gt; </span> <span>Specification/ Realization/ Other</span><span>, choose a property, and then select an element. </span></li><li><span>The element will be selected in the Containment tree. </span> </li></ol></div><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="traceability_properties_goto_menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in Go To submenu" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="440e93af-1b6b-44bb-ac54-f3301b3d5346">Traceability properties in the Go To submenu</ac:inline-comment-marker></h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712217 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01271: Traceability properties in notes

- page_id: `136712217`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712217/Traceability+properties+in+notes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:17.990+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Traceability relations representation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The traceability properties can be visualized on a diagram using the standard MagicDraw mechanism displaying the property values in notes.

To visualize an element’s traceability with other elements in the note on a diagram

1. Create a note for the element, whose traceability properties you want to visualize.
2. From the note’s shortcut menu, select Edit Compartments > Element Properties .
3. In the list on the left-hand side, click the traceability property of you choice (e.g., Realizing Class or Specifying Use Case) and then click the > button.
4. Click OK. Now you can view the element’s traceability with other elements. [ATTACHMENT filename='traceability_properties_in_notes.png'] Traceability properties in the notes on a diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>The traceability properties can be visualized on a diagram using the standard MagicDraw mechanism displaying the property values in notes. </span></p><p><span>To visualize an element’s traceability with other elements in the note on a diagram</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Create a note for the element, whose traceability properties you want to visualize.</li><li><span>From the note’s shortcut menu, select </span> <span>Edit Compartments </span> <span>&gt; </span> <span>Element Properties</span> <span>.</span></li><li><span>In the list on the left-hand side, click the traceability property of you choice (e.g., </span> <span>Realizing </span>Class or Specifying Use Case) and then click the &gt; button.</li><li><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>Click </span> <span>OK</span><span>. Now you can view the element’s traceability with other elements. </span></p></div></div> <ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="traceability_properties_in_notes.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Traceability properties in notes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><div class="layoutArea"><h6 class="column" style="text-align: center;"> Traceability properties in the notes on a diagram </h6></div></li></ol></div></div>
````

## PAGE 01272: Traceability properties in Properties panel

- page_id: `136712214`
- space_key: `MD2024x`

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Traceability** tab in the element’s **Properties** panel (at the bottom of the Browser window) is one of the places, wherein the element’s traceability properties, showing its realizing and/or more specific elements, are represented.

To view an element’s traceability properties in its **Properties** panel

1. Select the element in the Containment tree or its shape on the diagram.
2. In the Model Browser, click on the Properties panel > Traceability tab. Now you can view element’s traceability properties. [IMAGE alt='' src='']Traceability tab in the Properties panel

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712203 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01273: Traceability properties in Specification window

- page_id: `136712203`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712203/Traceability+properties+in+Specification+window
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:16.814+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability > Traceability relations representation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Traceability property group in element’s Specification window is one of the places, wherein the element’s traceability properties, showing its realizing and/or more specific elements, are represented.

To view an element’s traceability properties in its Specification window

1. Select an element and open the element’s Specification window by right clicking on the element.
2. Click the Traceability property group.
3. Now you can view element’s traceability properties. [IMAGE alt='' src='']Traceability property group in the Specification window

The following picture gives an example of the traceability relations between the elements from different abstraction levels of the same project.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of single level traceability relations

As you see, the “Create User” use case from the “Requirements” package is covered by two design classes, and these classes are accordingly specified by this particular use case.

Therefore, the traceability properties such as **Realizing Class and Realizing Element**represent both design classes as realizing elements of this use case. Accordingly, the traceability properties such as **Specifying Use Case**of each design class represent the “Create User” use case as a more specific element.

Note that the same related element can be represented through different traceability properties.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Realizing and Specifying traceability properties in Specification window

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

