<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715353 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01293: UML metamodel changes

- page_id: `136715353`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715353/UML+metamodel+changes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:24.323+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Plugins migration > Plugins migration to MagicDraw 15.0 and later Open API
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are two types of changes in UML metamodel API.

**UM****L specification changes**

The UML 2.1.2 (2.2) specification introduced few changes in the UML metamodel itself. Some metamodel classes were added, some of them were removed, some metaclasses properties were changed. Most of these changes are not in a core UML, so they will affect you if your plugin is oriented to complex things in UML.

Most changes are made in UML Templates, Simple Time, Interactions. We suggest to look at UML 2.1.2 (2.2) specification for such changes if you see some compile errors.

**UM****L metamodel API implementation changes**

Earlier UML metamodel API used several interface layers for every compatibility level described in the UML specification. All these layers were merged into one using package merging, this is why earlier API had so many interfaces for every metamodel element in a different package. Such structure was very complicated and hardly understandable for new UML users.

MagicDraw version 15.0 and later UML metamodel API provides just one final merged layer. All intermediate layers were dropped from API. This change reduced a UML metamodel API size in few times.

Interfaces that are removed form the following packages:

- com.nomagic.uml2.ext.omg

- com.nomagic.uml2.magicdraw

- com.nomagic.uml2.omg

We left the same package name for merged interfaces like in a previous API version, that is *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.*********. You do not need to make any changes in your code if you were using interfaces from this layer. Otherwise you need to import a statement in your java source files, for example, *import**com.nomagic.uml2.omg**.kernel.Element*to *import com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element*.

UML metamodel interfaces use Java 5 generics, so it is much easier to understand types of various collections in the metamodel.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>There are two types of changes in UML metamodel API.</p><p><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">UM</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">L specification changes</strong></p><p>The UML 2.1.2 (2.2) specification introduced few changes in the UML metamodel itself. Some metamodel classes were added, some of them were removed, some metaclasses properties were changed. Most of these changes are not in a core UML, so they will affect you if your plugin is oriented to complex things in UML. </p><p>Most changes are made in UML Templates, Simple Time, Interactions. We suggest to look at UML 2.1.2 (2.2) specification for such changes if you see some compile errors.</p><p> </p><p><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">UM</strong><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">L metamodel API implementation changes</strong></p><p>Earlier UML metamodel API used several interface layers for every compatibility level described in the UML specification. All these layers were merged into one using package merging, this is why earlier API had so many interfaces for every metamodel element in a different package. Such structure was very complicated and hardly understandable for new UML users. </p><p>MagicDraw version 15.0 and later UML metamodel API provides just one final merged layer. All intermediate layers were dropped from API. This change reduced a UML metamodel API size in few times.</p><p>Interfaces that are removed form the following packages:</p><ul><li><em>com.nomagic.uml2.ext.omg</em></li></ul><ul><li><em>com.nomagic.uml2.magicdraw</em></li></ul><ul><li><em>com.nomagic.uml2.omg</em> </li></ul><p>We left the same package name for merged interfaces like in a previous API version, that is <em>com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.</em><strong><em>** </em></strong>. You do not need to make any changes in your code if you were using interfaces from this layer. Otherwise you need to import a statement in your java source files, for example, <em>import </em><em>com.nomagic.uml2.omg</em><em>.kernel.Element </em>to <em>import com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element</em>.</p><p>UML metamodel interfaces use Java 5 generics, so it is much easier to understand types of various collections in the metamodel.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715139 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01294: UML model

- page_id: `136715139`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715139/UML+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:33:51.810+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide
- labels: ['omg-uml-metamodel', 'element', 'base-element']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In projects created with our modeling tools the UML model is an implementation of the OMG UML 2.5.1 metamodel. We do not provide a very detailed description of all UML metamodel elements and their properties in this documentation or javadoc. Our UML implementation covers whole UML specification as its and also extends it by introducing some additional Elements (like Diagram). You can find all this information in the UML 2.5.1 superstructure specification on the Object Management Group official Web site at http://www.omg.org/spec/UML/.

You should use UML model interfaces from the *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw*package.

Never use implementation classes like com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.impl.ElementImpl in your code!

The base interface of all model classes is *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element*, it implements the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.BaseElement* inteface.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

All structure derived from the *Element*you can see in the OMG-UML 2.5.1 Superstructure Specification.

All attributes and references described in the UML specification are implemented and accessible through setters and getters.

The UML model is thread safe for reading, but still the **UML model writing (modification) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Multi-threading' space='']**.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>In projects created with our modeling tools the UML model is an implementation of the OMG UML 2.5.1 metamodel. We do not provide a very detailed description of all UML metamodel elements and their properties in this documentation or <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">javadoc</span>. Our UML implementation covers whole UML specification as its and also extends it by introducing some additional Elements (like Diagram). You can find all this information in the UML 2.5.1 superstructure specification on the Object Management Group official Web site at http://www.omg.org/spec/UML/.</p><p>You should use UML model interfaces from the <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw </em>package.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a7779025-6848-45b6-ba02-5330a975ffbf"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Never use implementation classes like com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.impl.ElementImpl in your code!</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The base interface of all model classes is <em>com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element</em>, it implements the <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715188 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
````
## PAGE 01295: UML model implementation in 17.0.1 and up details

- page_id: `136715188`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715188/UML+model+implementation+in+17.0.1+and+up+details
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:29:12.609+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > UML model > UML Model Implementation Using EMF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In version 17.0.1 of MagicDraw (or other modeling tool developed by us), the UML metamodel has only one package - UML, and all UML metadata exist in this package. All UML metadata can be found in *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.metadata.UMLPackage*. For example:

```java

```

UML model elements can be created using the EMF factory *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.metadata.UMLFactory*. For example:

```java

```

In version 17.0.1, an element created using the EMF factory will be placed in the repository that does not belong to any project. The created object will be automatically added into the project’s repository after it will be added into the container that is in the project’s repository.

All UML model elements are directly or indirectly contained by EMF resources. Resource of an element can be retrieved in the standard way:

```java

```

There is no strict definition how EMF resources are organized in MagicDraw project. It depends on the project type (local or from the Teamwork Cloud server).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In version 17.0.1 of MagicDraw (or other modeling tool developed by us), the UML metamodel has only one package - UML, and all UML metadata exist in this package. All UML metadata can be found in <em>com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.metadata.UMLPackage</em>. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1db420e1-89f5-41ea-a07a-52ebf32eed6f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[EAttribute nameAttribute = UMLPackage.Literals.NAMED_ELEMENT__NAME;]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>UML model elements can be created using the EMF factory <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.metadata.UMLFactory</em>. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f2ec01bd-1123-49c3-9259-e771c4cc9c80"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Component component = UMLFactory.eINSTANCE.createComponent();]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In version 17.0.1, an element created using the EMF factory will be placed in the repository that does not belong to any project. The created object will be automatically added into the project’s repository after it will be added into the container that is in the project’s repository.</p><p>All UML model elements are directly or indirectly contained by EMF resources. Resource of an element can be retrieved in the standard way:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba770e0c-7b9e-4cf1-8f5f-35a04d29e3ee"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Element element;
org.eclipse.emf.ecore.resource.Resource resource = element.eResource();]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>There is no strict definition how EMF resources are organized in MagicDraw project. It depends on the project type (local or from the Teamwork Cloud server). </p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715187 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01296: UML model implementation in version 17.0 details

- page_id: `136715187`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715187/UML+model+implementation+in+version+17.0+details
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:11.470+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > UML model > UML Model Implementation Using EMF
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The MagicDraw (or other modeling tool developed by us) 17.0 UML metamodel has a nested package structure. UML is a root package that has nested subpackages, for example, classes/mdkernel, components/mdbasiccomponents, mdusecases. Metadata about a specific model element can be found in the package of the element. For example, a UML model element *Property*is from the package *uml/classes/mdkernel*, so metadata about *Property*can be found in the package *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.metadata.MdkernelPackage*.

```java

```

Model elements can be created using the EMF UML factory *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.metadata.UMLFactory*: For example:

```java

```

The element will be created in the repository of the active project. UML model elements are not contained by any EMF resource.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="WordSection1"><p>The MagicDraw  (or other modeling tool developed by us) 17.0 UML metamodel has a nested package structure. UML is a root package that has nested subpackages, for example, classes/mdkernel, components/mdbasiccomponents, mdusecases. Metadata about a specific model element can be found in the package of the element. For example, a UML model element <em>Property </em>is from the package <em>uml/classes/mdkernel</em>, so metadata about <em>Property </em>can be found in the package <em>com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.metadata.MdkernelPackage</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="16b492e9-24a0-4813-919c-268a9a8195c6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[EAttribute nameAttribute = UMLPackage.Literals.NAMED_ELEMENT__NAME;]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Model elements can be created using the EMF UML factory <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.metadata.UMLFactory</em>: For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2d59b466-59fc-4a77-ab1c-35971a01e854"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Component component = UMLFactory.eINSTANCE.createComponent();]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The element will be created in the repository of the active project. UML model elements are not contained by any EMF resource.</p></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715186 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01297: UML Model Implementation Using EMF

- page_id: `136715186`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715186/UML+Model+Implementation+Using+EMF
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:11.405+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > UML model
- labels: ['uml-model']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Starting from version 17.0, a UML model is implemented using Eclipse Modeling Framework (EMF), that is the UML model is an EMF model. All UML model classes implement the *org.eclipse.emf.ecore.EObject* interface. The UML model can be accessed and changed using the EMF API. For example:

```java

```

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Starting from version 17.0, a UML model is implemented using Eclipse Modeling Framework (EMF), that is the UML model is an EMF model. All UML model classes implement the <em>org.eclipse.emf.ecore.EObject</em> interface. The UML model can be accessed and changed using the EMF API. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4a0b1bbe-90bf-4573-9e51-51f77796eef7"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    // get project model
    Package model = project.getPrimaryModel();
    // create session
    SessionManager.getInstance().createSession(project, "create class");


    // get name attribute
    EAttribute element_name = UMLPackage.eINSTANCE.getNamedElement_Name();
    // get name value (same as model.getName())
    Object name = model.eGet(element_name);
    System.out.println("name = " + name);
    // change name value (same as model.setName(name + "_1");)
    model.eSet(element_name, name + "_1");


    Class aClass = UMLFactory.eINSTANCE.createClass();


    // get packaged element collection
    Collection collection = (Collection)
    model.eGet(UMLPackage.eINSTANCE.getPackage_PackagedElement());
    // add new class (same result as model.getPackagedElement().add(aClass))
    collection.add(aClass);


    // close session
    SessionManager.getInstance().closeSession(project);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9af33752-130c-44db-a684-a1347acc778b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="aca68646-5aeb-4b2c-99bf-d4236f7b83b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715175 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01298: UML model properties names

- page_id: `136715175`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715175/UML+model+properties+names
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:50:05.242+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > UML model > Event support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Constants for UML model properties names are defined in several classes.

Use*com.nomagic.uml2.impl.PropertyNames* for standart UML metamodel property names.

Use*com.nomagic.uml2.impl.PrivatePropertyNames*for auto-generated opposite property names.

The events’ names related with model creation/ deletion are listed in the*com.nomagic.uml2.ext.jmi.UML2MetamodelConstants*class.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Constants for UML model properties names are defined in several classes.</p><p><br /></p><p><span style="color: black;">Use </span><span style="color: black;"><em>com.nomagic.uml2.impl.PropertyNames</em> for standart UML metamodel property names.</span></p><p><span style="color: black;">Use </span><em style="color: black;line-height: 1.4285715;">com.nomagic.uml2.impl.PrivatePropertyNames</em><span style="color: black;"> for auto-generated opposite property names. </span></p><p><span style="line-height: 1.4285715;"><br /></span></p><p><span style="line-height: 1.4285715;">The events’ names related with model creation/ deletion are listed in the </span><em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">com.nomagic.uml2.ext.jmi.UML2MetamodelConstants</em><span style="line-height: 1.4285715;"> class.</span></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714816 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01299: UML Profiling and DSL Guide

- page_id: `136714816`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714816/UML+Profiling+and+DSL+Guide
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:50.226+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation
- labels: ['uml-profiling', 'dsl']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

UML is a general purpose visual modeling language for specifying, constructing and documenting the artifacts of systems to be used with all major application domains and implementation platforms. It has been widely adopted by both industry and academia as the standard language for describing software systems.

The MagicDraw UML tool provides the ability to use the DSL engine to adapt domain-specific profiles, enabling you to create your own custom diagrams, custom specification dialogs, custom real-time semantic rules, etc. In other words, you can create a low-budget specialized domain-specific tool and hide the UML underneath.

DSL customization is a model-driven approach, based on UML profiling.

##### Demo

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true2

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><p>UML is a general purpose visual modeling language for specifying, constructing and documenting the artifacts of systems to be used with all major application domains and implementation platforms. It has been widely adopted by both industry and academia as the standard language for describing software systems.</p><p>The MagicDraw UML tool provides the ability to use the DSL engine to adapt domain-specific profiles, enabling you to create your own custom diagrams, custom specification dialogs, custom real-time semantic rules, etc. In other words, you can create a low-budget specialized domain-specific tool and hide the UML underneath. </p><p>DSL customization is a model-driven approach, based on UML profiling.</p><p><br /></p><h3>Demo</h3><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e2e531c5-b0c3-42fa-b577-fcf8c98ac6de"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ha89QJzS9nk" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36e7271c-b9d7-41c4-9ef6-042156269856"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="d002a359-0c0f-4a70-be74-b108116c4a43"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="depth">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709881 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01301: Understanding cell content

- page_id: `136709881`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709881/Understanding+cell+content
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:12.382+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Analysis diagrams > Dependency Matrix > Using Dependency Matrix
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Tip

309059529

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Tip

309059531

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Tip

309059530

This page explains the content of the cell in the Dependency matrix. The following sections describe the Dependency matrix areas and cell content:

43

##### Rows, columns, and cells in the Dependency matrix

The Dependency matrix contains the following areas:

- Rows display elements specified in the Row Element Type box and Row Scope box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space='MD2024x'] .
- Columns display elements specified in the Column Element Type box and Column Scope box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space='MD2024x'] .
- *Cells* display dependencies between elements specified in the rows and columns. The content of the cells depends on selected criteria in the **Dependency Criteria** box and the **Direction** box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space='MD2024x']. Tip[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting criteria' space='MD2024x']]]> [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The main areas of the Dependency matrix. The illustration displays concepts from the SysML Plugin.

##### Representation of the dependencies in a cell

The default representation of the dependencies in the cells includes the following:

- Arrowed diagonal . The direction of the arrow is from the client element to the supplier element.

#### NOTE: Arrowed diagonal representation

Arrowed diagonal representation

The representation of the arrowed diagonal is different when the same elements are connected through:

- One dependency , e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='one_way_arrow.png'] .
- Two dependencies, e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='two_arrows.png'] and [ATTACHMENT filename='two_arrows_different_direction.png'] .
- More than two dependencies, e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='one_way_arrow_with_number.png'] , [ATTACHMENT filename='two_way_arrow_with_number.png'] .

- Diagonal . Elements depend on each other.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

You can specify the colors and line styles used to represent the relationships in your dependency matrices. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying relationship line styles' space='MD2024x']]]>

##### Numbers in the Dependency matrix

There are three types of numbers in the matrix (see the following figure):

1. The number of dependencies in the row.
2. The number of dependencies in the column.
3. The number of criteria in the cell.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### The details of a cell

Move your mouse over a cell to view details of the dependency (relationship) represented in the cell. A tooltip displays the information about the relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The ToolTip displays information about existing relations between Over Pressure Valve and Anto Black-flow Valve Blocks. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.

309059528

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Dependency Matrix' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Dependency Matrix' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix environment' space='MD2024x']

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the**dependencyMatrixSample**model. Download[ATTACHMENT filename='dependencyMatrixSample.mdzip'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="411c8661-66be-460f-aee5-f98d4aafbbce"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">309059529</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="398ebca7-2396-43b9-8caa-614c4d3b90da"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">309059531</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5a55e734-7084-47f5-a3db-9de99ff034f9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">309059530</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This page explains the content of the cell in the Dependency matrix. The following sections describe the Dependency matrix areas and cell content:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="45d947e0-2bb0-4e01-a024-e523f693e115"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><h3>Rows, columns, and cells in the Dependency matrix</h3><p>The Dependency matrix contains the following areas:</p><ul><li><em>Rows</em> display elements specified in the <strong>Row Element Type</strong> box and <strong>Row Scope</strong> box of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency Matrix Criteria area" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[criteria area]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><em>Columns</em> display elements specified in the <strong>Column Element Type</strong> box and <strong>Column Scope</strong> box of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency Matrix Criteria area" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[criteria area]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><em>Cells</em> display dependencies between elements specified in the rows and columns. The content of the cells depends on selected criteria in the <strong>Dependency Criteria</strong> box and the <strong>Direction</strong> box of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency Matrix Criteria area" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[criteria area]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="987ddde4-f9f4-4390-a376-fe719c729b3e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Selecting criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to specify boxes in the criteria area of the Dependency matrix >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dependency_matrix_area.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;">The main areas of the Dependency matrix. The illustration displays concepts from the SysML Plugin.</h6><h3>Representation of the dependencies in a cell</h3><p>The default representation of the dependencies in the cells includes the following:</p><ul><li><strong>Arrowed diagonal</strong>. The direction of the arrow is from the client element to the supplier element.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b9b1bbf7-8054-4c67-b330-eb956f91e1d3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Arrowed diagonal representation</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The representation of the arrowed diagonal is different when the same elements are connected through:</p><ul style="list-style-type: square;"><li>One dependency , e.g., <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="one_way_arrow.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li>Two dependencies, e.g., <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="two_arrows.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> and <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="two_arrows_different_direction.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li>More than two dependencies, e.g., <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="one_way_arrow_with_number.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> , <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="two_way_arrow_with_number.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><strong>Diagonal</strong>. Elements depend on each other.<br /><br /></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="063709c3-edc4-48bc-a7e5-2ca3ffbb11c1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can specify the colors and line styles used to represent the relationships in your dependency matrices. <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specifying relationship line styles" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to specify relationship line styles >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Numbers in the Dependency matrix</h3><p>There are three types of numbers in the matrix (see the following figure):</p><ol><li>The number of dependencies in the row.</li><li>The number of dependencies in the column.</li><li>The number of criteria in the cell.</li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="numbers_in_dependency_matrix.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>The details of a cell</h3><p>Move your mouse over a cell to view details of the dependency (relationship) represented in the cell. A tooltip displays the information about the relationships.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="tooltip_in_dependency_matrix.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding cell content" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The ToolTip displays information about existing relations between Over Pressure Valve and Anto Black-flow Valve Blocks. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b21eae9-c61e-4cd5-bc3e-440a343ec652"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">309059528</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating Dependency Matrix" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using Dependency Matrix" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency Matrix environment" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><span><strong>Sample model</strong><br /></span></p><p><span>The model used in the figures of this page is the </span><strong>dependencyMatrixSample</strong><span> model. Download </span><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="dependencyMatrixSample.mdzip"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link><span>.</span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711902 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01302: Understanding change concept

- page_id: `136711902`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711902/Understanding+change+concept
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:00.976+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Merge
- labels: ['change-concept', 'change-type', 'addition-change', 'deletion-change', 'modification-change', 'movement-change', 'order-change', 'change-states', 'dependent-changes', 'conflicting-changes', 'equivalent-changes']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1993842782

INLINE

1993842784

INLINE

1993842783

INLINE

Change is a difference, found between the ancestor and a contributor. Every change can be accepted or rejected, as well as have dependent changes. Changes can conflict with each other or be equivalent.

Merging begins with building a composite change tree, which consists of a model, diagramming, and non-model changes.

##### **Change types**

Read the following definitions to get familiar with different change types.

**#additionchangeAddition change** 
If an element has been added to a contributor, an addition change occurs.

**#deletionchangeDeletion change** 
If an element has been removed from a contributor, a deletion change occurs.

**#modificationchangeModification change** 
If an element property in a contributor has been modified, a modification change occurs.

If the **Is****Abstract** property value of a class in the ancestor had the default value *false* and the same property value in a contributor has been changed to *true*, a modification change occurs.

There are three types of modification changes:

- Addition modification change that occurs when a value is added to a property.
- Deletion modification change that occurs when a value is removed from a property.
- Replacement modification change that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.

****

**#movementchangeMovement change**

If an element owner has been changed in a contributor, a movement change occurs.

Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and package B contains the same class in a contributor. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the contributor. This case is recognized as a movement change.

Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the ancestor, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a contributor.

****

**#orderchangeOrder change** 
If the order of elements has been changed in a contributor, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection. 
 
Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.

Let's say class A has attributed a, b, and c in the ancestor. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a contributor. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the contributor. This is a case of the order change.

**Turn off order change detection**

This is the optional phase of the merge procedure. You may not need to perform it.

Order changes can be skipped while merging. For this, you need to specify names of properties wherein order changes should not be detected. 
 
To turn off the order changes detection in specificproperties

1. Fromthe Options menu select Environment . The Environment Options dialog opens.
2. Find the Do Not Detect Order Changes for the option under the Merge category in the General options group.
3. In the option value cell, specify names of properties wherein order changes should not be detected while merging.

Property names must be written in camel case, for example, ownedAttribute, ownedElement, and so on.

**#changestatesChange states**

Every change, whether it is addition, modification, deletion, movement, or order change, can be either accepted or rejected. It is a change state.

All accepted changes will be incorporated into the target. Alternatively, they can be rejected and excluded from the target.

****

**#dependentchangesDependent changes**

In some cases, other changes have to be accepted or rejected before accepting or rejecting a selected change. In other words, a selected change sometimes depends on other changes and is called a dependent change.

For a better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.

Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type. This means that type creation change must be accepted before accepting type modification change.

Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a contributor. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the contributor. In this case, three changes occur:

- deletion change (for the old type)
- addition change (for the new type)
- modification change (for the property type)

These are also ownership changes, but they are accepted together with deletion and addition changes. 
 The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.Thus accepting the deletion change means also accepting the addition change, the modification change and the deletion change itself.

****

**Conflicting changes**

Conflict occurs when two changes are incompatible, i.e., the changes cannot be accepted together. Conflicts can only occur when using 3-way merge.

The following situations will result in a conflict:

- Each contributor has changed a class name or any other element property value.
- One contributor has added an operation to a class and the other contributor deleted the class.
- One contributor has moved a class into one package while the other contributor moved it to another package.

****

**#equivalentchangesEquivalent changes**

Equivalent change is a pair of identical changes that are detected in both source and target when performing a 3-way merge.

Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and both contributors contain the same class in package B. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in both source and target. This is a case of the equivalent change.

Another case of equivalent changes is when the same element A becomes renamed to B in both contributors.

****

**Direct and All changes**

Diagrams that are loaded and contain changes are considered to be directly modified by the user. In the**Environment Options**dialog, you can select whether to analyze the changes in all diagramsin the model (*All changes*)or limit your investigation to diagrams that were loaded (open) when the changes were made (*Direct Changes*).

To specify which changes to detect in diagrams

1. From the Options menu select Environment . The Environment Options dialog opens.
2. Under General > Merge and Compare , find Detect Changes in Diagrams .
3. Select either Direct Changes or All Changes .

Direct Changes

All Changes

1993842779

In other words, a selected change sometimes depends on other changes and is called a dependent change.</p><p>For a better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e231999-6ec7-43d1-aa0a-7bbbb69b45ae"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the contributor. In this case, three changes occur:</p><ul><li>deletion change (for the old type)</li><li>addition change (for the new type)</li><li>modification change (for the property type)</li></ul><p>These are also ownership changes, but they are accepted together with deletion and addition changes.<br /> The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.Thus accepting the deletion change means also accepting the addition change, the modification change and the deletion change itself.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Conflicting changes</strong></p><p>Conflict occurs when two changes are incompatible, i.e., the changes cannot be accepted together. Conflicts can only occur when using 3-way merge.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de1e0e95-13ee-45c3-8c87-f1c412e9bfaf"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following situations will result in a conflict:</p><ul><li>Each contributor has changed a class name or any other element property value.</li><li>One contributor has added an operation to a class and the other contributor deleted the class.</li><li>One contributor has moved a class into one package while the other contributor moved it to another package.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22c0bb61-77bd-4811-a52d-71f398b03479"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#equivalentchanges</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Equivalent changes</strong></p><p>Equivalent change is a pair of identical changes that are detected in both source and target when performing a 3-way merge.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d46f7e7-ee62-4949-9153-a75ff54171b5"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and both contributors contain the same class in package B. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in both source and target. This is a case of the equivalent change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e60837bb-231f-40fe-afd6-272ff1def8c4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Another case of equivalent changes is when the same element A becomes renamed to B in both contributors.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><br /></strong></p><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Direct and All changes</strong></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Diagrams that are loaded and contain changes are considered to be directly modified by the user. In the </span><strong>Environment Options</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> dialog, you can select whether to analyze the changes in all diagrams</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> in the model <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">(</span><em>All changes</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">) </span>or limit your investigation to diagrams that were loaded (open) when the changes were made (</span><em>Direct Changes</em><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">)</span>.</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify which changes to detect in diagrams</p><hr /><ol><li>From the <strong>Options</strong> menu select <strong>Environment</strong>. The<strong> Environment Options</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>Under <strong>General</strong> &gt; <strong>Merge and Compare</strong>, find <strong>Detect Changes in Diagrams</strong>.</li><li>Select either <strong>Direct Changes</strong> or <strong>All Changes</strong>.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e6e307e9-6cd3-4b82-b059-1f6c22bbaed9"><ac:rich-text-body>The <strong>Direct Changes</strong> option is specified by default, meaning that only the changes made in the loaded diagram (changes users are aware of) are compared. To do a full comparison of the selected project versions, change the option to <strong>All Changes</strong>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40ebb9c5-81d3-489b-9bd7-9191c5deafa9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1993842779</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro conf-macro output-block"><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding merge types" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Preparing for merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Starting Model Merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Analyzing and managing merge results" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Finishing and canceling merge" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712737 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01303: Understanding change types

- page_id: `136712737`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712737/Understanding+change+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:49.455+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Comparing projects
- labels: ['change-concept', 'addition-change', 'deletion-change', 'modification-change', 'movement-change', 'order-change', 'dependent-changes', 'change-types']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Change is a difference, found between the base and compared project versions.

Read the following definitions to get familiar with different change types.

**#additionchangeAddition change** 
If an element has been added to a compared project version, an addition change occurs.

**#deletionchangeDeletion change** 
If an element has been removed from a compared project version, a deletion change occurs.

**#modificationchangeModification change** 
If an element property in a compared project version has been modified, a modification change occurs.

If the **Is****Abstract** property value of a class in the base had the default value *false* and the same property value in a compared project version has been changed to *true*, a modification change occurs.

There are three types of modification changes:

- **Addition modification change** that occurs when a value is added to a property.
- **Deletion modification change** that occurs when a value is removed from a property.
- **Replacement modification change** that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.

****

**#movementchangeMovement change**

If an element owner has been changed in a compared project version, a movement change occurs.

Let's say package A contains some class in the base and package B contains the same class in a compared project version. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the compared project version. This case is recognized as a movement change.

Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the base, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a compared project version.

****

**#orderchangeOrder change** 
If the order of elements has been changed in a compared project version, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection. 
 
Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.

Let's say class A has attributes a, b, and c in the base. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a compared project version. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the compared project version. This is a case of the order change.

Order changes can be skipped while comparing projects. For this you need to specify names of properties wherein order changes should not be detected. 
 
 To turn off the order changes detection in specific properties

1. From the **Options** menu select **Environment**. The **Environment Options** dialog opens.
2. Find the **Do Not Detect Order Changes for** option under the **Merge** **and Compare** category in the **General** options group.
3. In the option value cell, specify names of properties wherein order changes should not be detected while merging.

Property names must be written in camel case, for example, ownedAttribute, ownedElement, and so on.

**Dependent change**

#dependentchangeIn some cases, changes depend on other changes and are called dependent changes.

For better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.

Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type.

Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a compared project version. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the compared project version. In this case, three changes occur:

- deletion change (for the old type)
- addition change (for the new type)
- modification change (for the property type)

The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.

#directandallchanges**Direct and All changes**

Diagrams that are loaded and contain changes are considered to be directly modified by the user. In the **Environment Options** dialog, you can select whether to analyze the changes in all diagrams in the model (*All changes*) or limit your investigation to diagrams that were loaded (open) when the changes were made (*Direct Changes*).

To specify which changes to detect in diagrams

1. From the Options menu select Environment . The Environment Options dialog opens.
2. Under General > Merge and Compare , find Detect Changes in Diagrams .
3. Select either Direct Changes or All Changes .

Direct Changes

All Changes

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136713371 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01304: Understanding changes

- page_id: `136713371`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136713371/Understanding+changes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:15:30.455+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud > Used Project Switch Impact Analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following definitions show the different change types.

**Addition change** 
In the context of used project impact analysis, an addition change is treated to be the one when an element is restored from a recovered state to a normal one. Such element change is represented in the green rectanglein the **Affected elements** panel.

**Deletion change** 
If an element has been removed, a deletion change occurs. The deletion change usually results in composition integrity errors, i.e., it causes the appearance of recovered elements, since the deleted element is still used in the main project.

**Modification change** 
If an element property has been modified, a modification change occurs.

**Understanding element marking**

When a change occurs on a single element, the element marking indicates the state of the change which can be either accepted or rejected.

| Marking | Change state | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (green tick before the element icon) | Accepted | A new element had been created in one of the contributors and its addition was accepted. |
| (red cross before the element icon) | Rejected | A new element had been created in one of the contributors and its addition was rejected. |

To understand the concept of change states, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Elements with resolved conflicting changes are decorated as shown in the following table. Owners of these elements are also decorated.

| Marking | Change applied by | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (red diamond on the lower-left corner of the element icon) | System | The element had a conflicting modification change which was resolved automatically. |
| (yellow diamond on the lower-left corner of the element icon) | User | The element had a conflicting modification change which was resolved by the user. |

**Recognizing addition changes**

For the addition change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Added elements are highlighted in green background. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing deletion changes**

For the deletion change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Deleted elements are highlighted in red background.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

****

**Recognizing modification changes**

For the modification change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the **Specification** panel.

For more information about using the Specification panel, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Inspecting changes in the Change details panel' space='MD2024x'].

**Recognizing movement changes**

For the movement change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes. Since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with an arrow symbol on the left.

The following figure illustrates a movement change, when element X has been moved from package A to package B. The movement change had been accepted and element X became owned by package B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can navigate from the element original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.

**Recognizing order changes**

For the order change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. 
After accepting the change, all the elements in the collection are reordered so that it would be the same as in one of the contributors.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can turn off the order changes detection. For more about it, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Finishing and canceling merge' space='MD2024x']

**Recognizing elements with changed owned elements** 
Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlighted using red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Modified elements with changed owned elements are highlighted in blue with red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">The following definitions show the different change types. </span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Addition change</strong></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">In the context of used project impact analysis, an addition change is treated to be the one when an element is restored from a recovered state to a normal one. Such element change is represented in the green rectangle</span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> in the <strong>Affected elements</strong> panel.<br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Deletion change</strong></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">If an element has been removed, a deletion change occurs. The deletion change <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">usually results in composition integrity errors, i.e., it causes the appearance of recovered elements, since the deleted element is still used in the main project.</span><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Modification change</strong></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">If an element property has been modified, a modification change occurs.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Understanding element marking</strong></span></p><p>When a change occurs on a single element, the element marking indicates the state of the change which can be either accepted or rejected.</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p>Marking</p></th><th><p><strong>Change state</strong></p></th><th><p><strong>Example</strong></p></th></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="green_tick.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> (green tick before the element icon)</p></div></td><td><p>Accepted</p></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="accepted_change.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> A new element had been ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing deletion /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Deletion change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Deleted elements are highlighted in red background.</span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:image ac:title="Example of deletion change" ac:alt="Example of deletion change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deletion_change.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><br /></strong></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing modification changes</strong></span></p><p>For the modification change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="modificationchange"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding change concept" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Modification change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="element_modified_properties.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br /><br />To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the <strong>Specification</strong> panel.</p><p>For more information about using the Specification panel, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inspecting changes in the Change details panel" /></ac:link>.</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing movement changes</strong></span></p><p>For the movement change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="movementchange"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding change concept" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Movement change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes. Since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with an arrow symbol on the left.</p><p>The following figure illustrates a movement change, when element X has been moved from package A to package B. The movement change had been accepted and element X became owned by package B.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of accepted movement change" ac:alt="Example of accepted movement change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="accepted_movement_change.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>You can navigate from the element original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing order changes</strong></span></p><p>For the order change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="orderc"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding change concept" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Order change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. <br class="atl-forced-newline" />After accepting the change, all the elements in the collection are reordered so that it would be the same as in one of the contributors.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of accepted order change" ac:alt="Example of accepted order change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_order_change.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>You can turn off the order changes detection. For more about it, see <ac:link ac:anchor="diagramannotationson"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Finishing and canceling merge" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Turning on showing merged diagram annotations.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing elements with changed owned elements</strong> </span><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlighted using red diagonals.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Element with owned elements added" ac:alt="Element with owned elements added"><ri:attachment ri:filename="owned-elements-added.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />Modified elements with changed owned elements are highlighted in blue with red diagonals.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Element with owned elements added and modification change" ac:alt="Element with owned elements added and modification change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="modified-owned-elements.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding changes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712747 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01305: Understanding content of the Differences tree

- page_id: `136712747`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712747/Understanding+content+of+the+Differences+tree
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:50.283+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Comparing projects > Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['element-decorations', 'recognizing-changes', 'comparison-result']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Elements with all change types are displayed in the **Differences**tree that is located at the top left of the **Difference Viewer** window.

**Used project representation**

This node indicates that the compared project contains modified used projects or used projects with modified usages.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing addition changes**

For the addition change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space='MD2024x'].

Added elements are highlighted in green background. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing deletion changes**

For the deletion change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space='MD2024x'].

Deleted elements are highlighted in red background. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing modification changes**

For the modification change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space='MD2024x'].

Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the **Specification** panel.

For more information about using the Specification panel, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Inspecting differences in the Change details panel' space='MD2024x'].

**Recognizing movement changes**

For the movement change description, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space='MD2024x'].

Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with arrow symbol on the left.

The following figure illustrates a movement change, when element X has been moved from package A to package B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can navigate from the element's original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
**Recognizing order changes**

For the order change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space='MD2024x'].

Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing elements with changed owned elements** 
Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlighted using red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Modified elements with changed owned elements are highlighted in blue with red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711926 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01306: Understanding content of the Merged Result panel

- page_id: `136711926`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711926/Understanding+content+of+the+Merged+Result+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:01.728+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Merge > Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['merged-result', 'element-decorations', 'change-states', 'recognizing-changes']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

177724953

INLINE

177724956

INLINE

177724954

INLINE

Elements with all change types are displayed in the **Merged Result** tree/list that is located at the top left of the **Merge** window.

You can filter the elements displayed in the **Merged Results** tree/list by type of changes. Use the **Changes filter** drop-down list to filter out a specific type of changes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

By default, the changes from the Target and equivalent changes (changes made both in Target and Source) are automatically hidden from the **Merge** **Result** tree/list, meaning that only the Source changes are shown. This allows reducingthe number of changes needed to review for the user. If the filter is applied, the following notification appears:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Understanding element marking**

When a change occurs on a single element, the ticks and crossesbefore the element icon indicate the state of the change which can be either accepted or rejected.

| Marking | Change state | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (green tick before the element icon) | Accepted | A new element had been created in one of the contributors and its addition was accepted. |
| (red cross before the element icon) | Rejected | A new element had been created in one of the contributors and its addition was rejected. |

To understand the concept of change states, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x']Change states.

Elements with resolved conflicting changes are marked as shown in the following table. Owners of these elements are also marked.

| Marking | Change applied by | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (red diamond on the lower-left corner of the element icon) | System | The element had a conflicting modification change which was resolved automatically. |
| (yellow diamond on the lower-left corner of the element icon) | User | The element had a conflicting modification change which was resolved by the user. |

**Recognizing addition changes**

For the addition change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Added elements are highlighted in green background. 
 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing deletion changes**

For the deletion change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Deleted elements are highlighted in red background.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

****

**Recognizing modification changes**

For the modification change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. 
 [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the **Specification** panel.

For more information about using the Specification panel, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Inspecting changes in the Change details panel' space='MD2024x'].

**Recognizing movement changes**

For the movement change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes. Since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with an arrow symbol on the left.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can navigate from the element original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.

**Recognizing order changes**

For the order change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. 
After accepting the change, all the elements in the collection are reordered so that it would be the same as in one of the contributors.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can turn off the order changes detection. For more about it, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Finishing and canceling merge' space='MD2024x']

**Recognizing elements with changed owned elements** 
Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlighted using red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

177724951

INLINE

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting merge summary information and changes legend' space='MD2024x']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying and navigating through changes' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Quickly navigating through conflicting changes' space='MD2024x']
