From the shortcut menu select <strong>Element</strong><strong>Numbering</strong>. The <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Element Numbering dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Element Numbering</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> opens.</li><li>In the list on the right side of the dialog, select all leaves and/or nodes, and then click <strong>Create</strong>.<ul><li>To select all leaves and/or nodes, click the first leaf or node in the list and then hold down the Shift key when selecting the last leaf or node in the list.</li></ul></li><li>All the leaves and/or nodes become numbered.</li><li>Change the order of leaves and/or nodes in the tree by changing their positions in the list appropriately.<ul><li>To move a leaf or a node/leaf up, select the row and click the <strong>Decrease</strong> button. </li><li>To move a leaf or a node/leaf down, select the row and click the <strong>Increase</strong> button.</li></ul></li></ol><h3>Nesting</h3><p>You can move and arrange User Interface Modeling elements since they nest each other, and create deep structured User Interface models. However, not all elements have the nesting ability: only <strong>Container</strong> elements can nest other elements. All frames nest in each other. As a result, if you were to move the element with title <em>Frame 1</em>, all other elements would stay in position within this element. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="359" ac:width="464"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavb48f115e0ff673ee99f82c420118b5a6.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a deep structure that was created using containers</h6><h3>Reusability</h3><p>If you have created a complex model and need to use it again, you do not have to create a new one. All you need to do is select the elements, copy them and paste them into the same diagram or in any other diagram. You can also reuse a single element by copying and pasting it or by dragging it from the Browser onto the diagram pane. </p><h3>Specifying elements</h3><p>You can modify UI modeling elements by editing their properties in the following three ways: </p><ul><li>Via the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specification window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li><li>Via the shortcut menu. </li><li>On a diagram pane.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To edit properties via the Specification window</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Right-click on it and select <strong>Specification</strong> on the shortcut menu.</li><li>Edit one of the properties to modify the element.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="443" ac:width="544"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddava5e1fce994dba1b3b20391944e4f227f.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Specification dialog box of the Slider element</h6><p>The so-called boolean properties that have only two values can also be edited via the shortcut menu. </p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="999b5aad-6e80-4040-904c-9b973e57e015"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following instructions are for boolean properties ONLY. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To edit properties via the shortcut menu</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Right-click it. The shortcut menu will appear and the editable properties will be displayed at the bottom.</li><li>Select the property you want to change. </li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="322" ac:width="398"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavbffe5c4d37f6f962fdc3946caf66d50e.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Shortcut menu of the Slider element with boolean properties</h6><p>For the elements that own a text or a title (such as a button or a frame), it is also possible to edit the appropriate properties straight on a diagram.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fcb05e07-0728-48cb-8a1c-493942d052e1"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following instructions are for the text or title properties ONLY.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To edit the element properties on a diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Click the selected element.</li><li>Type a text on the element (see the following figure).</li><li>Press ENTER. The text you have typed will appear on the element and in the <strong>Text</strong>/<strong>Title </strong>property of the element as well.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="47" ac:width="120"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav11240983e48d0b0469795a548c6e0311.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Editing title of element</h6><h3>Icon usage</h3><p>Some elements are capable of owning an icon. User Interface Modeling provides a number of frequently-used icons, such as cut, delete, undo/redo, etc. Of course, it is also possible to use images from your computer as icons.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add an icon</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element that can own an icon, such as a button.</li><li>Right-click the element and select <strong>Specification</strong> in the shortcut menu. </li><li>In the <strong>Icon</strong> list, select the icon you want to appear on the element.<ul><li>If you want to use an image from your computer as an icon, click <strong>custom</strong>. </li></ul></li></ol><h3 style="text-align: left;">Using symbol properties</h3><p>User Interface Modeling also allows you to modify User Interface elements via <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[symbol properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><p>To edit an element via symbol properties</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element on the diagram.</li><li>Right-click and select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> on the shortcut menu.</li><li>Change one of the properties listed in the table below. For example, if you want to set the same background color to more than one UI modeling element, you can select all these elements in the diagram and then change the required property for all of them in the same way.</li></ol><p>The following table lists the supported symbol properties:</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Symbol Property</strong></p></td><td><p><strong>Effect</strong></p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Fill Color</p></td><td><p>Sets the background color of the component to the chosen color; the property <strong>Use Fill Color</strong> must be marked.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Font</p></td><td><p>Sets the font if there is any text in the component.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Text Color</p></td><td><p>Sets the text color of the component to the chosen color.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Pen Color</p></td><td><p>Sets the color of the border for components that have a color.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><h3>User interface prototyping</h3><p>User Interface Modeling allows you to create browsable reports. You can create a hyperlink from the UI model element to the report. Once a report of these models has been created, click on any element in the report. You will then be directed to the other diagram. When you click <strong>OK</strong> in the upper left-hand frame you will be directed to the next frame. After clicking <strong>OK</strong> in that frame, you can get to a couple of frames from which you can step forward and backward as you want, as they are all linked to one another.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ac:link> </span>provides an example of how this feature works and explains how to add hyperlinks and create browsable reports.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="UI_workflow.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Example of User Interface Models and their Workflow</span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="92e50e5a-94ff-44f6-a790-a3f9fc311376"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">3452345879</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related References:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Extension diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extension diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="e6cecee9-2173-4632-9ca4-e9e0f7a9866a"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="User Interface Modeling diagram" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710001 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01340: User Interface Modeling diagram

- page_id: `136710001`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710001/User+Interface+Modeling+diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T15:03:13.487+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Extension diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1192686252

INLINE

100.00002%

262346576

INLINE

234523468

INLINE

A User Interface (UI) Modeling diagram makes it possible to build prototypes of user interfaces, connect UI mockups with the whole Architectural model, export them as images, and create browsable reports for presentations. In short, they help gather information faster and thus save time and money.

The advantage of User Interface Modeling lies in its ability to:

- Create WYSIWYG User Interface prototypes rapidly.
- Integrate User Interface development with UML specifications.
- Get immediate feedback from prospective users on real situations and reuse it for future designs.
- Create browsable reports with the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard' space='MD2024x'].

****

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of User Interface diagram Model for *Calculator*

****

373920617

**Related pages**

- UML diagrams
- Extension diagram

****

**Sample m****odel**

A Web UI sample project, representing the UI Modeling Diagram usage for web-based interface modeling, is available at <*MagicDraw* 
*installation directory>\samples\diagrams\User* Interface Modeling.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136713495 space=MD2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 01341: Using a 3DEXPERIENCE server project in your project

- page_id: `136713495`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136713495/Using+a+3DEXPERIENCE+server+project+in+your+project
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2023-12-14T14:44:54.053+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Collaboration powered by 3DEXPERIENCE platform > Managing project usages via 3DEXPERIENCE platform
- labels: ['indirectly-used-project', 'directly-used-project']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Used project

761447331

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Used project

761447334

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Used project

761447332

If you want to use an element from either a server or a local project, you must use that project in your project first. Projects can be used only in the read-only mode.

#### NOTE: Using a local project in your project

Using a local project in your project

To start using a local project in your project, add the local project to the server first by choosing **Collaborate > Add Project to Server…**

##### Using a server project in your project

To start using a server project in your project

1. Select one of the following:
  - Options > Project Usages
  - Collaborate > Project Usages > Project usages The Project Usages dialog opens.
2. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='add_server_project_button.png'] button and then select Use Server Project (see the following figure). The Use Server Project wizard opens . [ATTACHMENT filename='project_usages.png']
3. Select the server project you want to start using and click **Next**. Directly/Indirectly used projectsThe selected project is treated as a directly used project. A project used by a used project is treated as an indirectly used project. Search barUse the search bar to filter projects according to:Project iterationAuthorComment
4. Select a version of the server project to use and click Finish . The wizard closes.
5. Wait while the usage to the selected project is established.
6. View the selected server project added to the usages of your project In the **Project Usages** dialog. 
 
The used project version shows which version is used, as well as the latest project version (for example, [1] A.1/[1] A.2 means project version [1] A.1 is used, but version [1] A.2 is the latest project version). 
Select **Notify on Any New Version** to get instant notifications about changes to used projects to ensure they are always up to date. Used projectPlease note that the entire contents of a used project are added to the main project.
7. Click OK to close the dialog.

The selected server project also appears in the Model Browser, under the package *Project Usages*.

Once the usage to the project is established, you can start using its elements. For example, you can select them as types in your project.

761447330

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing used projects in 3DEXPERIENCE' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Changing used project version in 3DEXPERIENCE' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stop using 3DEXPERIENCE project in your project' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Projects can be used only in the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5a87190c-e9eb-4f01-a4ee-c9bc1729727d">read-only</ac:inline-comment-marker> mode.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6be39c1d-637f-4afe-b018-9121f8306cb1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Using a local project in your project</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To start using a local project in your project, add the local project to the server first by choosing <strong>Collaborate &gt; Add Project to Server… </strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Using a server project in your project</h3><p>To start using a server project in your project</p><hr /><ol><li>Select one of the following:<ul><li><strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project Usages</strong></li><li><strong>Collaborate</strong> &gt; <strong>Project Usages</strong> &gt; <strong>Project usages</strong><br />The <strong>Project Usages</strong> dialog opens.<br /><br /></li></ul></li><li>Click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="17"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_server_project_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2022x" ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button and then select <strong>Use Server Project</strong> (see the following figure). The <strong>Use Server Project</strong> wizard <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="46e90495-c7db-4427-8db3-dfe6e531e691">opens</ac:inline-comment-marker>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="project_usages.png" /></ac:image></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Select the server project you want to start using and click <strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="ffbd09a6-29f2-4a61-a65a-5b85fcbda24f">Next</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong>.<br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="188537b7-0b11-49db-a363-8615da04fc08"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Directly/Indirectly used projects</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The selected project is treated as a directly used project. A project used by a used project is treated as an indirectly used project.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5c459004-01a4-49e1-a5cd-e9e03857bb9d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Search bar</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Use the search bar to filter projects according to:</p><ul><li>Project iteration</li><li>Author</li><li>Comment</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Select a version of the server project to use and click <strong>Finish</strong>. The wizard <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="97e31ec1-db0c-4349-a4dd-d0dab85672a4">closes.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li>Wait while the usage to the selected project is established.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">View the selected server project added to the usages of your project In the <strong>Project Usages</strong> dialog.<br /><br />The used project <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="af0f1f4d-77d2-40cd-8845-c7837c829c5c">version</ac:inline-comment-marker> shows which version is used, as well as the latest project version (for example, [1] A.1/[1] A.2 means project version [1] A.1 is used, but version [1] A.2 is the latest project version). <br />Select <strong>Notify on Any New Version</strong> to get instant <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6529d724-9516-40c1-9429-5e9f2669b58c">notifications</ac:inline-comment-marker> about changes to used projects to ensure they are always up to <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="9fa75bb5-ecfd-467a-9ca8-031ebee2c39c">date</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06fe1728-d632-460c-878d-d8b4e6697ee7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Used project</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Please note that the entire contents of a used project are added to the main project.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the dialog.</li></ol><p>The selected server project also appears in the Model Browser, under the package <em>Project Usages</em>.</p><p>Once the usage to the project is established, you can start using its elements. For example, you can select them as types in your project.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a737b25d-1986-4fb5-a03a-a42ac14746eb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">761447330</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Editing used projects in 3DEXPERIENCE" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Changing used project version in 3DEXPERIENCE" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Stop using 3DEXPERIENCE project in your project" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

Open API exposes some reusable and customizable UI components. They can be used for example from custom actions.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715757 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01343: Using code completion to develop BeanShell scripts

- page_id: `136715757`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715757/Using+code+completion+to+develop+BeanShell+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:43.909+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use any text editor to develop a scripting language. However, a standard text editor lacks of code assistant features. Most scripting languages are loose type. For example, to define a variable in JavaScript, you need to type:

```text

```

It is difficult to determine what type of "a" later in the source code. With BeanShell, you can use a variable without declaring it, for example:

```text

```

Or you can declare it first:

```text

```

Java IDE does not officially support code completion for scripting languages. However, there is a workaround if you use BeanShell. First you need a Java IDE. If you do not have one, you can select NetBeans because it has the smallest file size. You can download the latest version of NetBeans at *[http://www.netbeans.org/downloads/index.html](http://www.netbeans.org/downloads/index.html)*. The NetBeans Java SE package is enough. Second you need to set up a MagicDraw classpath.

To set up a classpath point in the MagicDraw library in NetBeans

1. Click Tools > Libraries on the main menu. The Library Manager dialog will open.
2. Click the New Library button. The New Library dialog will open.
3. Specify a library name, for example, MD16.6. The Library type must be Class Libraries .
4. Click OK to close the New Library dialog.
5. Select your new library in the Libraries tree.
6. Click the Add JAR/Folder button and add all the JAR files in <MagicDraw>/lib .
7. Click OK to close the Library Manager dialog.

To develop a BeanShell script in NetBeans

1. Click File > New Project on the main menu to create a Java application project. The New Project dialog will open.
2. Select Java in the Categories box and Java Application in the Projects box, and then follow the instructions.
3. Expand your project node in the Project window. The Libraries node will appear.
4. Right-click the Libraries node and select Add Library from the shortcut menu.
5. Select the MagicDraw library that you have previously created (see "To set up a classpath point in the MagicDraw library in NetBeans:") and click Add Library .
6. Click File > New File on the main menu to add a new Java file.
7. Select Java in the Categories box and Empty Java File in the File Types box, and then follow the instructions until finish.

You need to create a public static method in a Java file, for example, main() method, to follow the standard Java programming language. At the end of the file, insert a statement to call the static method. See the example in *create_project_elements.bsh* in the *<MagicDraw>/samples/product features/macros* directory.

The official filename extension for BeanShell is*****.bsh*. However, you can add a*****.java*file to the BeanShell scripting language.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can use any text editor to develop a scripting language. However, a standard text editor lacks of code assistant features. Most scripting languages are loose type. For example, to define a variable in JavaScript, you need to type:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="869cb462-7271-436c-a1e0-4675c9823359"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[var a;]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> </p><p>It is difficult to determine what type of &quot;a&quot; later in the source code. With BeanShell, you can use a variable without declaring it, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e3fc647f-10fd-4b2f-8751-0fd5810f5c3c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[a = new File("file.txt");]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> </p><p>Or you can declare it first:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4a2a4d8c-2a75-43a4-96fe-541c9837d22b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[File a = new File("file.txt");]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> </p><p>Java IDE does not officially support code completion for scripting languages.  However, there is a workaround if you use BeanShell. First you need a Java IDE. If you do not have one, you can select NetBeans because it has the smallest file size. You can download the latest version of NetBeans at <em><a class="external-link" href="http://www.netbeans.org/downloads/index.html" rel="nofollow">http://www.netbeans.org/downloads/index.html</a></em>. The NetBeans Java SE package is enough. Second you need to set up a MagicDraw classpath.</p><p> </p><p>To set up a classpath point in the MagicDraw library in NetBeans</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Libraries</strong> on the main menu. The <strong>Library Manager</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Click the <strong>New Library</strong> button. The<strong> New Library</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Specify a library name, for example, MD16.6. The Library type must be <strong>Class Libraries</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>New Library</strong> dialog.</li><li>Select your new library in the Libraries tree.</li><li>Click the <strong>Add JAR/Folder</strong> button and add all the JAR files in <strong>&lt;MagicDraw&gt;/lib</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the <strong>Library Manager</strong> dialog.</li></ol><p> </p><p>To develop a BeanShell script in NetBeans</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New Project</strong> on the main menu to create a Java application project. The <strong>New Project</strong> dialog will open.</li><li>Select <strong>Java</strong> in the<strong> Categories</strong> box and <strong>Java Application</strong> in the <strong>Projects</strong> box, and then follow the instructions.</li><li>Expand your project node in the <strong>Project</strong> window. The Libraries node will appear.</li><li>Right-click the Libraries node and select <strong>Add Library</strong> from the shortcut menu.</li><li>Select the MagicDraw library that you have previously created (see &quot;To set up a classpath point in the MagicDraw library in NetBeans:&quot;) and click <strong>Add Library</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>New File</strong> on the main menu to add a new Java file.</li><li>Select <strong>Java</strong> in the <strong>Categories</strong> box and <strong>Empty Java File</strong> in the <strong>File Types</strong> box, and then follow the instructions until finish.</li></ol><p><br />You need to create a public static method in a Java file, for example, main() method, to follow the standard Java programming language. At the end of the file, insert a statement to call the static method. See the example in <em>create_project_elements.bsh</em> in the <em>&lt;MagicDraw&gt;/samples/product features/macros</em> directory.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc020c8f-2194-447b-be18-9eb848572e7c"><ac:rich-text-body><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><span>The official filename extension for BeanShell is</span><strong> </strong><em>.bsh</em><span>. However, you can add a</span><strong> </strong><em>.java</em><span> file to the BeanShell scripting language.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710491 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01346: Using diagram tabs

- page_id: `136710491`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710491/Using+diagram+tabs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:41.429+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

568043094

568043096

568043095

To split diagrams in new horizontal or vertical group

1. Do one of the following:
  - Right-click the diagram tab. The shortcut menu opens.
  - Select the open diagram tab, then drag it to the diagram pane. The shortcut menu opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='diagram_tab_shortcut_on_dgr.png']
2. Select New Horizontal Group or New Vertical Group to split the diagram pane and have more than one diagram opened at the same time.

To split diagrams, more than one diagram should be open.

To turn the diagram window to floating

- From the diagram tab shortcut menu, select Floating .

To show the diagram owner on the diagram tab

1. Select a package and create a diagram in it.
2. Right-click anywhere on the diagram, and from the shortcut menu, select Diagram Properties .
3. Set the Show Owner property to true .

##### Showing diagrams in Full Screen

If you want to see your diagrams in full screen and work exclusively from the diagram, use the **Show Tabs in Full-Screen** functionality. In a full-screen mode, all necessary modeling commands are visible with an option to hide, and the Model Browser is in the auto-hide mode. You can also set the interface components to be displayed or hidden.

To turn on diagram full-screen mode, do one of the following

- Double-click the diagram tab.
- From the diagram shortcut menu, select Show Tabs in Full Screen .
- From the View menu, select Show Tabs in Full Screen .
- Press F11. You can change the **Show Tabs in Full Screen** shortcut key in the **Environment Options** dialog, **Keyboard** option group.

You can turn off the diagram full-screen mode in the same way as turning it on.

###### Managing Interface Components in Diagram Full Screen Mode

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x'] is in the auto-hide mode when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x']tabs are located on the left side of the window. To display the browser, move the mouse pointer over the desired browser window tab (for example, on the Containment tab), and the tab opens.

The main toolbar is hidden when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. To display the main toolbar, set the **Hide Toolbars in the Full Screen Mode** property to false in the **Environment Options** dialog, General pane, and **Display** option group.

The diagram toolbar is displayed when the diagram full-screen mode is turned on. Right-click the diagram toolbar to manage it.

568043093

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715759 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 01347: Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts

- page_id: `136715759`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715759/Using+Eclipse+to+develop+Groovy+scripts
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-01T13:53:06.901+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:

1. Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.
2. Develop a Groovy script.

To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin

1. Go to [https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse](https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse) .
2. Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.

To develop a Groovy Script

1. Create a Groovy project.
2. Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click Properties .
3. Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the Libraries tab on the right pane.
4. Click Add External JARs to add all the JAR files in <modeling tool name>/lib and in <modeling tool name>/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar .
5. Click OK to close the dialog.
6. Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Use the following three simple steps to develop and run a Groovy script in NetBeans:</p><ol><li>Install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li><li>Develop a Groovy script.</li></ol><p>To install Groovy-Eclipse Plugin</p><hr /><ol><li>Go to <em><a href="https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse">https://github.com/groovy/groovy-eclipse</a></em>.</li><li>Follow the instructions for installing Groovy-Eclipse Plugin.</li></ol><p>To develop a Groovy Script</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a Groovy project.</li><li>Right-click the project in Package Explorer and click <strong>Properties</strong>.</li><li>Click Java Build Path in the tree on the left-hand side. Click the <strong>Libraries</strong> tab on the right pane.</li><li>Click <strong>Add External JARs</strong> to add all the JAR files in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/lib</em> and in <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;/plugins/com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton/engines/groovy-2.0.1/embeddable/groovy-all-2.0.1.jar</em>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> to close the dialog.</li><li>Create a new Groovy class in the project. Remove a class declaration, and then put the script there.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712339 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 01348: Using Find operation

- page_id: `136712339`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712339/Using+Find+operation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:27.783+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Useful information

68780004

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Useful information

68780016

68780003

**On this page**

****

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Useful information

68780006

MagicDraw utilizes a Find operation that allows you to automatically search for model elements, represent relations between these elements, or gather their [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom traceability rules' space='MD2024x'] values each time you update your model. The Find operation is customizable, so you can change the default values of all the properties for the selected operation at any any time. The Find operation can be accessed and used:

- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Relation Map' space='MD2024x'] to represent relations between elements.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix' space='MD2024x'] to associate elements in rows and columns of a matrix.
- From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart Package' space='MD2024x'] to query a model and gather specific elements.
- From the Specification window of the selected element to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic table' space='MD2024x'] to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.
- From the Specification window of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating executable opaque behaviors' space='MD2024x'] to specify the body of a StructuredExpression.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

If you would like to simply find the desired model element, and don't need to automatically query your model every time you update it, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Find dialog' space='MD2024x']**Find** dialog instead.

To use a Find operation, you need to specify it as criteria. The next section describes how to complete this task.

##### Specifying a Find operation as criteria

Specifying a Find operation as criteria allows you to automatically query your model in order to find and render specific model elements, relations between these elements, or gather their derived properties depending on where the operation is used (as mentioned above).

To specify a Find operation as criteria

1. Open the dialog for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024x'] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the open dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Find_criterion.png']
3. Change the **Operation Name**, if needed.
4. Select the **Style** to represent the specified Find criteria. Note that the style selection is available only in the **Relation Criteria** and **Dependency Criteria** dialogs.
5. Specify the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Find dialog' space='MD2024x'] , and click OK .

#### TIP: Useful information

Useful information

Selecting the**Search in all texts** check box allows you to search for text in Documentation and TODO properties.

##### expertAdvanced Manipulations

###### Specifying the Find operation properties

To specify the Find operation properties

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024x'] .
2. On the left side of the dialog, expand the Find operation.
3. Select the property you want to specify, and, in the property specification panel on the right side of the dialog, specify the value of the selected property. [ATTACHMENT filename='find_relation_criteria_dialog.png']
4. Click OK . The selected Find operation is now customized to query a model according to your specified properties. You can use a specific Find operation as an argument of another operation. In other words, you can create a new Find, Filter, Implied Relation, Exclude, Script, Union or Collection operation, and use the results of a specific Find operation as its property.

###### **find as property**To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting started with specifying criteria' space='MD2024x'] .
2. Select Find on the left side of the dialog.
3. In the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog, click the **Use as** button, and select the desired command that corresponds to the operation you want to create as shown below. A new operation is created. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Specify other parameters of the created operation, and click OK . You now have a new operation that has been created with the selected Find operation as one of its properties.

68794211

**Related pages**

- Specifying criteria for querying model
  - Getting started with specifying criteria
  - Using Simple Navigation
  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Implied Relations
  - Creating new operations
    - Calling operations from the model
    - Creating script operations

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="231785dc-5bcb-4ad3-bcaa-bc987f91a085"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780004</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ad9c228-6cc2-4c56-b835-de1065ff6026"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780016</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="23823620-6e72-473a-a4eb-1649e7e581c3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780003</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9d9bd883-f80d-4224-8670-309ea8e2cad5" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fdf56894-d521-4b81-acf1-362fbbacb63f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68780006</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>MagicDraw utilizes a Find operation that allows you to automatically search for model elements, represent relations between these elements, or gather their <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[derived property]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> values each time you update your model. The Find operation is customizable, so you can change the default values of all the properties for the selected operation at any any time. The Find operation can be accessed and used:</p><ul><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Relation Map" /></ac:link> to represent relations between elements.</li><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency Matrix" /></ac:link> to associate elements in rows and columns of a matrix.</li><li>From the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> of a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Smart Package" /></ac:link> to query a model and gather specific elements.</li><li>From the Specification window of the selected element to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.</li><li>In a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Generic table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Generic Table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to calculate/gather the values of derived properties.</li><li>From the Specification window of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating executable opaque behaviors" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[opaque behavior]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to specify the body of a StructuredExpression.<br /> </li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="abe846db-615f-4e59-b8d6-be5f9b6795c5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you would like to simply find the desired model element, and don't need to automatically query your model every time you update it, use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Find dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Find</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> instead.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To use a Find operation, you need to specify it as criteria. The next section describes how to complete this task.</p><h3>Specifying a Find operation as criteria</h3><p>Specifying a Find operation as criteria allows you to automatically query your model in order to find and render specific model elements, relations between these elements, or gather their derived properties depending on where the operation is used (as mentioned above).</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify a Find operation as criteria</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the dialog for <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Select <strong>Find</strong> on the left side of the open dialog.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Find_criterion.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p>Change the <strong>Operation Name</strong>, if needed.</p></li><li><p>Select the <strong>Style</strong> to represent the specified Find criteria. Note that the style selection is available only in the <strong>Relation Criteria</strong> and <strong>Dependency Criteria</strong> dialogs.</p></li><li>Specify the <ac:link ac:anchor="Find dialog options"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Find dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Find operation properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /> </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d1e6a977-d642-493c-a2e9-f3cf1e6cdd46"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Useful information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Selecting the<strong> Search in all texts</strong> check box allows you to search for text in Documentation and TODO properties.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d1a126e-a626-422b-9c1f-995128cfe8a4"><ac:parameter ac:name="">expert</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Advanced Manipulations</h3><h4>Specifying the Find operation properties </h4><p>To specify the Find operation properties </p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the dialog for specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>On the left side of the dialog, expand the Find operation.</li><li>Select the property you want to specify, and, in the property specification panel on the right side of the dialog, specify the value of the selected property.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="find_relation_criteria_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />The selected Find operation is now customized to query a model according to your specified properties. You can use a specific Find operation as an argument of another operation. In other words, you can create a new Find, Filter, Implied Relation, Exclude, Script, Union or Collection operation, and use the results of a specific Find operation as its property. </li></ol><h4><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1dee633f-88ad-4f66-bb82-5e8dbb066561"><ac:parameter ac:name="">find as property</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation</h4><p>To use the expression of a Find operation as a property of another operation</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Getting started with specifying criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the dialog for specifying criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Select <strong>Find</strong> on the left side of the dialog.</li><li><p>In the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog, click the <strong>Use as</strong> button, and select the desired command that corresponds to the operation you want to create as shown below. A new operation is created.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Find_use_as.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using Find operation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li>Specify other parameters of the created operation, and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />You now have a new operation that has been created with the selected Find operation as one of its properties.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfb704e1-7e83-4c8d-bc46-19c1abd44a38"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">68794211</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Specifying+criteria+for+querying+model">Specifying criteria for querying model</a><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Getting+started+with+specifying+criteria">Getting started with specifying criteria</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+Simple+Navigation">Using Simple Navigation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+Metachain+Navigation">Using Metachain Navigation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+Implied+Relations">Using Implied Relations</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+new+operations">Creating new operations</a><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Calling+operations+from+the+model">Calling operations from the model</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+script+operations">Creating script operations</a></li></ul></li></ul></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````
